<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Internet of Things" as per the ITU-T Y.2060 recommendation. List any four enabling technologies of IoT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** As per ITU-T Y.2060, the Internet of Things is defined as *"a global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."*

**Four Enabling Technologies (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **RFID (Radio Frequency Identification):** Used for automatic object identification and tracking.
2. **WSN (Wireless Sensor Networks):** Network of spatially distributed sensors for environmental monitoring.
3. **Cloud Computing:** Provides scalable storage and computational resources.
4. **Big Data Analytics:** Processes massive volumes of IoT-generated data for insight extraction.

*(Other valid answers: NFC, Edge AI, LPWAN, 5G, Embedded Systems, Actuators.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of the "Middleware Layer" in the five-layer IoT architecture with a biomedical example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Middleware Layer** (also called Processing or Edge Layer) acts as the **intelligence hub** of an IoT system. Its key roles are:

1. **Data Aggregation (1 mark):** Collects data from multiple heterogeneous sensors and merges them into a unified format.
2. **Analytics and Decision-Making (1 mark):** Applies AI/ML models for inference, anomaly detection, and predictive analysis.
3. **Storage and Interoperability (1 mark):** Stores processed data in databases and translates between protocols (e.g., MQTT to HL7 FHIR).

**Biomedical Example:** In a remote patient monitoring system, the middleware layer processes raw ECG signals from a wearable patch, detects arrhythmias using a trained neural network, and pushes alerts to the cardiologist's dashboard within **< 100 ms**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **five-layer IoT reference architecture**. Explain the function of each layer in a smart health monitoring system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **three-layer** and **five-layer IoT architectural models**. Justify why the five-layer model is preferred for **biomedical IoT applications** with suitable reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) (7 marks):**

The five-layer IoT reference architecture consists of the following layers:

**1. Perception Layer (1 mark):** Contains physical sensors (ECG, SpO₂, temperature, accelerometer) that convert real-world physiological signals into electrical/digital form. They use **IEEE 802.15.4 / BLE** for short-range communication.

**2. Network Layer (1 mark):** Comprises gateways, routers, and wireless infrastructure (**Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRaWAN**). Transports the digitized sensor data to the processing layer using protocols like **MQTT** or **CoAP**.

**3. Middleware Layer (1.5 marks):** The processing brain. Performs **data filtering, aggregation, feature extraction, and AI-based inference**. Example: A fog node running a CNN model that classifies ECG beats as normal or arrhythmic.

**4. Application Layer (1.5 marks):** Delivers domain-specific services to end users. In healthcare, this could be a **doctor's mobile app** showing real-time vitals, or an **EMR-integrated patient portal**.

**5. Business Layer (2 marks):** Governs the entire system via business models, regulatory compliance (**HIPAA, GDPR, ABDM**), and revenue mechanisms. It uses **BI tools** to monitor KPIs such as alert-to-action time, patient outcomes, and cost reduction.

*(Block diagram should be drawn — refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Three-Layer Model | Five-Layer Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Layers** | Perception, Network, Application | Perception, Network, Middleware, Application, Business |
| **Processing** | Only at application end | Distributed (edge + cloud) |
| **Intelligence** | Limited | High (AI/ML integrated) |
| **Scalability** | Moderate | High |
| **Suitability for Medical IoT** | Poor | Excellent |

**Justification (3 marks):**

1. **Latency Sensitivity:** Biomedical applications (e.g., defibrillator, ICU monitor) require sub-**100 ms** latency. The five-layer model enables **edge computing** at the middleware layer, avoiding round-trip to a distant cloud.
2. **Data Heterogeneity:** Medical IoT involves multiple formats (HL7, DICOM, FHIR). The middleware layer handles **protocol translation and data normalization**, which a three-layer model cannot.
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Healthcare mandates strict adherence to **HIPAA, GDPR, and ABDM**. The dedicated business layer ensures compliance is **continuously monitored**, not bolted on as an afterthought.
4. **AI/ML Integration:** Modern clinical decision support requires AI inference. The middle layer in the five-layer model is the **natural home for ML models**, enabling real-time diagnosis.

**Conclusion:** Hence, the five-layer model is the **de-facto standard for biomedical IoT** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the **functional view of IoT** as per the ITU-T Y.2060 reference model. Explain the functions of the **Device**, **Communication**, **Service**, **Application**, and **Management** functional blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless body area network (WBAN) sensor transmits ECG data over a channel of bandwidth **B = 200 kHz** and **SNR = 12 dB**. Calculate the **maximum theoretical channel capacity** using the Shannon-Hartley theorem. If the actual achieved throughput is **70 %** of the theoretical capacity, find the **effective data rate** in kbps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) (7 marks):**

**Functional Blocks of IoT (ITU-T Y.2060):**

**1. Device Functional Block (1.5 marks):** Handles the **direct interaction with the physical world**. Contains sensors (ECG, SpO₂, glucose monitor) and actuators (insulin pump, defibrillator). Performs **sensing, identification, and actuation**.

**2. Communication Functional Block (1.5 marks):** Provides **connectivity** between devices and the service layer. Includes access networks (Wi-Fi, 5G, BLE), core networks, and gateways. Supports **IP-based and non-IP protocols**.

**3. Service Functional Block (1 mark):** Offers **service support and exposure functions** such as data storage, processing, device discovery, and QoS management. Acts as the bridge between the network and the application.

**4. Application Functional Block (1.5 marks):** Provides **domain-specific intelligence and interfaces** for end users. Examples: Smart health monitoring app, remote surgery platform, chronic disease management.

**5. Management Functional Block (1.5 marks):** Manages **device lifecycle, network performance, fault tolerance, and security** (authentication, encryption, key management). Ensures the system is **reliable, secure, and scalable**.

*(Block diagram should be drawn — refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth: $B = 200$ kHz
- SNR: $SNR_{dB} = 12$ dB
- Efficiency: $\eta = 70\% = 0.7$

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale (1 mark):**

$$
SNR_{linear} = 10^{\frac{12}{10}} = 10^{1.2}
$$

$$
10^{1.2} = 10 \times 10^{0.2} = 10 \times 1.5849 \approx 15.849
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem (2 marks):**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR_{linear})
$$

$$
C = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 15.849)
$$

$$
C = 200{,}000 \cdot \log_2(16.849)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\log_2(16.849)$ (1 mark):**

$$
\log_2(16.849) = \dfrac{\ln(16.849)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2.825}{0.6931} \approx 4.075 \;\; \text{bits/Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate theoretical capacity (1 mark):**

$$
C = 200{,}000 \times 4.075 = 815{,}000 \;\; \text{bps} = 815 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute effective data rate at 70 % efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
C_{eff} = \eta \times C = 0.7 \times 815 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

$$
C_{eff} = 570.5 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed for KTU valuation):**

$$
\boxed{C_{theoretical} = 815 \;\; \text{kbps}, \quad C_{effective} = 570.5 \;\; \text{kbps}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion of SNR** from dB to linear scale. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$** in Shannon's formula. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not specifying the difference between "three-layer" and "five-layer"** architectures. Marks deducted: **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing "Middleware" with "Application" layer** in diagrams. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Missing the role of the Business Layer** (regulatory compliance, ABDM) in biomedical IoT answers. Marks deducted: **1.5 marks**.
> 6. **Not drawing labeled block diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — IoT Architectural View**

- **Definition:** IoT = global infrastructure interconnecting physical/virtual things via interoperable ICT (ITU-T Y.2060).
- **Five Layers (in order):** Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business.
- **Three Layers (legacy):** Perception, Network, Application — **insufficient for biomedical IoT**.
- **Perception Layer:** Sensors + RFID + Actuators; energy-constrained; uses IEEE 802.15.4 / BLE.
- **Network Layer:** Gateways, routers, MQTT/CoAP/5G; **bridge to internet**.
- **Middleware Layer:** Edge AI, data fusion, HL7 FHIR translation; **smartest layer**.
- **Application Layer:** User-facing dashboards, alert systems, EMR integration.
- **Business Layer:** ABDM, HIPAA, GDPR, revenue models, BI analytics.
- **Functional View (Y.2060):** Device + Communication + Service + Application + Management blocks.
- **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR)$ — always convert SNR from **dB to linear** first.
- **SNR Conversion:** $SNR_{linear} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}$.
- **Latency Formula:** $L = t_{rx} - t_{tx}$; total latency = sum of all layer delays.
- **Throughput Formula:** $T = N \cdot S / t$ (packets × size / time).
- **PDR Formula:** $PDR = P_{received} / P_{sent} \times 100$.
- **Key Biomedical Example:** Wearable ECG → BLE → Edge AI → Doctor's App → ABDM EHR.
- **KPI for Clinical IoT:** Latency ≤ **100 ms**, Reliability (PDR) ≥ **99.9%**.
- **Emerging Trend:** **Edge/Fog computing** reduces cloud latency by up to **50–60%**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **neatly labeled five-layer block diagram** for full marks in 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Design of IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Design of IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The *Physical Design of IoT* refers to the hardware-level architecture of an IoT system. It defines the actual physical/electronic components — sensors, actuators, processors, transceivers, and power subsystems — that sense the environment, perform local computation, and transmit data over a network. The physical design is concerned with the *thing* (the device) itself, as opposed to the logical/functional design which describes data flow and services.

### 1.1 What is "Physical Design" of an IoT System?

When we say "Physical Design" in the IoT context, we are talking about the **hardware layer** of the IoT reference architecture. Just like a mobile phone has a physical body (screen, processor, battery, antenna), every IoT device has a physical layout of electronic components mounted on a Printed Circuit Board (PCB). The physical design answers questions such as:

- *What does the IoT device look like inside?*
- *Which sensors does it use?*
- *How does it talk to the internet?*
- *How is it powered?*
- *What enclosure protects it from heat, dust, or water?*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **Physical Design** → Hardware components, block diagram of the *thing*, signal acquisition, embedded design.
> - **Logical Design** → Functional blocks (Device, Network, Cloud, Application layers), data formats, APIs, services.
> - **Application Design** → User interfaces, business logic, dashboards, alert systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Wearable Watch

Imagine a **smart health watch** worn on your wrist. Look at its insides:

| Physical Element of the Watch | IoT Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Heart rate LED + photodiode | **Sensor** (senses pulse) |
| Tiny ARM microcontroller | **Processor / Embedded System** |
| Bluetooth antenna | **Communication Module (Transceiver)** |
| Lithium polymer battery | **Power Management Unit** |
| Vibration motor for alerts | **Actuator** |
| Watch strap + glass | **Enclosure / Casing** |
| Internal firmware | **Software / Middleware (not physical, but co-located)** |

So the "physical design" is essentially the **anatomy** of the IoT device. A biomedical IoT device (e.g., a wearable ECG patch) follows the *same* anatomy, just with medical-grade sensors.

### 1.3 The Standard Physical Block Diagram of an IoT Device

A generic IoT "thing" is composed of **five physical subsystems** (this is the answer that KTU examiners expect when they ask "Draw and explain the physical design of IoT"):

1. **Sensors / Actuators** — the *interface to the physical world*.
2. **Processor / Microcontroller** — the *brain* that runs firmware.
3. **Memory** — stores boot code, data, and logs.
4. **Communication Module (Transceiver)** — sends data to the gateway/cloud.
5. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** — supplies regulated voltage/current.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **S-P-M-C-P** → **S**ensor, **P**rocessor, **M**emory, **C**ommunication, **P**ower.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Metrics Used in Design

> [!NOTE]
> Standard engineering metrics every KTU student should commit to memory:
> - **Operating voltage** of common IoT MCUs: **3.3 V DC**.
> - **Battery cell nominal voltage (Li-Ion):** **3.7 V**.
> - **ISM bands for wireless IoT:** **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi/BLE/ZigBee), **868/915 MHz** (LoRa in EU/US), **433 MHz** (Sub-GHz industrial).
> - **ADC resolution benchmark:** **12-bit (4096 levels)** for medical-grade sensors.
> - **Typical current draw of a sleep-mode IoT node:** **< 10 µA** (microamperes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Subsystems — A Detailed Logical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Sensors
A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, SpO₂, ECG voltage) into a measurable electrical signal (analog voltage, current, or digital word).

**Working "Why & How":**
- *Why?* — IoT exists to digitize the physical world. Without sensors, there is no IoT.
- *How?* — Physical phenomenon → sensing element (e.g., thermistor, MEMS diaphragm) → analog electrical signal → front-end conditioning (amplifier/filter) → ADC inside the processor.

**Categories of Sensors in Biomedical IoT:**

| Category | Example | Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bioelectric** | ECG electrodes, EEG electrodes | Heart/brain signal acquisition |
| **Biochemical** | Glucose oxidase strip | Continuous blood glucose monitor (CGM) |
| **Biomechanical** | Strain gauge, accelerometer, gyroscope | Gait analysis, fall detection |
| **Optical** | Pulse oximeter (PPG) | SpO₂ and heart-rate |
| **Thermal** | Thermistor, RTD | Skin/body temperature |
| **Environmental** | Humidity, gas (MQ-series) | Ambient monitoring around the patient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors vs. Actuators** — A common KTU 2-mark question. A *sensor* senses, an *actuator* acts. Examples of actuators: DC motor, Peltier cooler, insulin pump stepper, vibrating pager, and a buzzer.

#### 2.1.2 Processor / Microcontroller (The "Brain")

The processor runs the embedded firmware that schedules sampling, performs local signal processing, and drives the transceiver. Choices in the IoT ecosystem include:

- **8-bit MCUs** (e.g., ATmega328 in Arduino Uno) — ultra-low-power, simple control loops.
- **32-bit ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4 MCUs** (STM32, nRF52, CC2538) — mainstream IoT.
- **System-on-Chip (SoC)** with integrated radio (ESP32, CC2650) — combines processor + transceiver on one die.
- **Edge-AI accelerators** (e.g., MAX78000, Kendryte K210) — on-device neural inference.

**Why does the choice matter?**
- Clock speed → determines **sampling rate**.
- Active current (mA) vs. sleep current (µA) → determines **battery life**.
- On-chip peripherals (ADC, I²C, SPI, DMA) → determines **sensor interfacing options**.

#### 2.1.3 Memory Subsystem

Three tiers of memory live inside the IoT device:

| Tier | Type | Purpose | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boot / Firmware** | NOR Flash, ROM | Stores program code | 256 KB – 4 MB |
| **Working Memory** | SRAM | Runtime variables, stack, buffers | 32 KB – 512 KB |
| **Data Logging** | NAND Flash, EEPROM, SD card | Buffered data when offline | 4 MB – 32 GB |

> [!NOTE]
> For medical IoT devices, **non-volatile buffering** is mandatory so that critical patient data is not lost during network dropouts (a KTU 7-mark elaboration point).

#### 2.1.4 Communication Module (Transceiver)

The transceiver is the *antenna + modem* that connects the device to the wider world. Choice depends on **range, data rate, and power budget**.

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Use in Biomedical IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)** | ~10 m | 1–2 Mbps | Low | Wearables (smart watch, CGM) |
| **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** | ~50 m | Up to 600 Mbps | High | In-hospital bedside monitors |
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | ~100 m | 250 kbps | Very low | Body Area Networks (BAN) |
| **LoRaWAN** | 2–10 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Very low | Remote patient monitoring in villages |
| **NB-IoT / LTE-M** | Cellular | 100–300 kbps | Medium | Ambulance-to-hospital telemetry |
| **NFC / RFID** | < 10 cm | < 424 kbps | Passive | Smart pill bottles, patient wristbands |

> [!TIP]
> KTU "Design Selection" Question Trick — Always justify by **Power–Range–Bandwidth** triangle. There is no single "best" protocol; the *application* dictates the choice.

#### 2.1.5 Power Management Unit (PMU)

PMU includes:
- **Source:** Li-Ion, Li-Po, coin cell (CR2032), energy harvester (solar, thermoelectric, piezoelectric).
- **Regulator:** LDO or buck converter that converts battery voltage to a stable **3.3 V** rail.
- **Supervisory circuitry:** Battery gauge, brown-out detector, sleep controller.

**Why it matters:** In wireless biomedical sensors (e.g., implantable loop recorder), battery replacement requires surgery, so **average power consumption** is the single most important design constraint.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although the physical design is largely architectural, a few quantitative relationships are repeatedly tested in KTU papers. The following table collects them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the LaTeX delimiters below only inside markdown (no vertical pipe characters `|` inside table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead).

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | $V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}-1} \cdot D$ | ADC output code $D$ to voltage conversion; $n$ = ADC resolution bits. Used to compute sensor measurement resolution. |
| F2 | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n}}$ | Smallest detectable voltage step (LSB) of an ADC. |
| F3 | $I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{\text{active}} \cdot t_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot t_{\text{off}}}{t_{\text{on}} + t_{\text{off}}}$ | **Duty-cycled average current** — fundamental battery-life equation for IoT nodes. |
| F4 | $t_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$ | Battery lifetime in hours; $C_{\text{bat}}$ in mAh, $I_{\text{avg}}$ in mA. |
| F5 | $P_{\text{total}} = V_{\text{dd}} \cdot \sum I_i$ | Total power budget — sum of sensor, MCU, radio currents. |
| F6 | $E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy in joules consumed in a duty cycle window. |
| F7 | $R_{\text{max}} = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4 \pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r}{P_r}}$ | Friis equation (qualitative) — link budget; explains why **2.4 GHz** has shorter range than **868 MHz**. |
| F8 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}$ | Sampling theorem — biomedical signals: ECG ~ 500 Hz, EEG ~ 250 Hz, body temperature ~ 1 Hz. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The physical design is the *only* layer that the patient or clinician physically interacts with. Good physical design in biomedical IoT translates directly into:

- **Patient safety** (low-voltage operation, isolated sensor front-ends, biocompatible enclosure).
- **Wearability** (flexible PCBs, skin-safe adhesives for wearables).
- **Reliability** (ingress protection — IP67 rating for sweat/water resistance).
- **Regulatory compliance** (IEC 60601 for medical electrical equipment).
- **Battery life** (which determines how often the patient must charge/replace the device — a major UX factor).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This topic is primarily architectural, but KTU frequently asks for **derivations of key physical-design equations** such as ADC resolution, duty-cycled battery life, and link-budget basics. The two derivations below are the most commonly expected.

### 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution and LSB Voltage (for a Biomedical Sensor Front-End)

**Problem Context:** A wearable pulse-oximeter uses a 12-bit ADC with a full-scale voltage $V_{\text{FS}} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. Compute the LSB voltage and the digital code corresponding to a measured PPG voltage of $1.85 \text{ V}$.

**Step 1 — Define the ADC full-scale range.**
An $n$-bit ADC divides $V_{\text{FS}}$ into $2^{n} - 1$ equal steps. The number of steps is one less than the number of codes because the zero code is included.

$$N_{\text{steps}} = 2^{n} - 1$$

For $n = 12$:

$$N_{\text{steps}} = 2^{12} - 1 = 4096 - 1 = 4095$$

**Step 2 — Compute the LSB (least-significant-bit) voltage.**
The LSB is the smallest voltage change that the ADC can resolve.

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{N_{\text{steps}}}$$

Substituting $V_{\text{FS}} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ and $N_{\text{steps}} = 4095$:

$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{4095}$$

$$\Delta V = 8.058 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \approx 805.8 \text{ µV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each LSB of this ADC corresponds to about **0.806 mV** — a figure that determines the smallest detectable PPG change in a pulse oximeter.

**Step 3 — Compute the digital output code for a measured $V_{\text{in}} = 1.85 \text{ V}$.**
Using the encoding relation:

$$D = \text{round}\!\left( \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{\Delta V} \right)$$

$$D = \text{round}\!\left( \frac{1.85 \text{ V}}{8.058 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}} \right)$$

$$D = \text{round}(2295.8) = 2296$$

In binary, $2296_{10} = 1000\,1111\,1000_2$.

**Step 4 — Verify by reverse conversion.**

$$V_{\text{out,calc}} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n}-1} \cdot D$$

$$V_{\text{out,calc}} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \cdot 2296$$

$$V_{\text{out,calc}} = 1.8505 \text{ V}$$

The quantization error is $1.8505 - 1.85 = +0.5 \text{ mV}$, which is well within $\pm 0.5 \cdot \Delta V$, confirming the calculation is correct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Halving the LSB (better resolution) is achieved by either increasing $n$ (more bits) or reducing $V_{\text{FS}}$ (using an instrumentation amplifier with gain). For ECG signals (≈ 1 mV), a gain of ~1000 is applied *before* the ADC so that the LSB represents a clinically meaningful voltage step.

### 3.2 Derivation: Battery Life with Duty-Cycled Operation

**Problem Context:** A wearable ECG patch is active for $t_{\text{on}} = 10 \text{ ms}$ to sample and transmit, then sleeps for $t_{\text{off}} = 990 \text{ ms}$. Active current $I_{\text{act}} = 25 \text{ mA}$, sleep current $I_{\text{slp}} = 5 \text{ µA}$, battery capacity $C_{\text{bat}} = 300 \text{ mAh}$. Find the average current and the battery life.

**Step 1 — Total cycle period.**

$$T = t_{\text{on}} + t_{\text{off}} = 10 \text{ ms} + 990 \text{ ms} = 1000 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the duty-cycled average-current formula.**

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{\text{act}} \cdot t_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{slp}} \cdot t_{\text{off}}}{T}$$

Convert all quantities to consistent units (seconds and amperes).

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A})(10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}) + (5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A})(990 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s})}{1 \text{ s}}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ C} + 4.95 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}}{1 \text{ s}}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 2.5495 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} \approx 254.95 \text{ µA} = 0.255 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the battery lifetime in hours.**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{300 \text{ mAh}}{0.255 \text{ mA}}$$

$$t_{\text{life}} \approx 1176.5 \text{ h} \approx 49.0 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check — what if the device stayed always-on?**

$$I_{\text{always}} = 25 \text{ mA} \;\Rightarrow\; t_{\text{life,always}} = \frac{300}{25} = 12 \text{ h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1 % duty cycle extended battery life by a factor of **~98×**. This single calculation is *the* reason biomedical wearables use aggressive sleep modes.

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Protocol for Which Biomedical Use?"

| Use-Case | Critical Parameter | Recommended Physical-Layer Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous glucose monitor (CGM) | Ultra-low power, body-worn | **BLE** | Phone-paired, 1-week battery, low data rate. |
| Implantable cardiac monitor (ICM) | < 1 µA sleep, short range | **Body-coupled comm. / MICS band (402–405 MHz)** | Penetrates tissue, regulated for implants. |
| Ambulance ECG to hospital | Wide-area, mobile | **LTE / 5G** | High mobility, QoS, low latency. |
| Rural village ASHA worker | Long range, low data | **LoRaWAN** | 10 km, < 50 mA peaks. |
| Smart ICU bed | High bandwidth, fixed | **Wi-Fi 6** | Carries video + vitals. |
| Patient wristband ID | Tap-to-read | **NFC / RFID** | No battery, very cheap. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IoT Device (Physical Design)

This Mermaid diagram represents the **physical hardware architecture** that KTU students are expected to draw in the 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV["PHYSICAL WORLD / PATIENT"]
        A1["Temperature"]
        A2["ECG Signal"]
        A3["SpO2 / PPG"]
        A4["Motion"]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["SENSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1["Analog Sensor 1"]
        B2["Analog Sensor 2"]
        B3["Analog Sensor 3"]
        B4["IMU / Accelerometer"]
    end

    subgraph FRONT["FRONT-END CONDITIONING"]
        C1["Amplifier + Filter"]
        C2["ADC 12-bit"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1["ARM Cortex-M MCU"]
        D2["Firmware / RTOS"]
        D3["Local Signal Processing"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1["SRAM"]
        E2["Flash"]
        E3["EEPROM / Log Buffer"]
    end

    subgraph COMM["COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        F1["Radio Transceiver"]
        F2["Antenna 2.4 GHz"]
        F3["Protocol Stack BLE / Wi-Fi / LoRa"]
    end

    subgraph POW["POWER MANAGEMENT"]
        G1["Li-Ion Battery"]
        G2["Voltage Regulator 3V3"]
        G3["Sleep Controller"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        H1["Buzzer / Vibrator"]
        H2["Peltier / Heater"]
        H3["Drug Pump Driver"]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> C2 --> D1
    A2 --> B2 --> C1
    A3 --> B3 --> C1
    A4 --> B4 --> D1
    D1 <--> D2
    D1 <--> D3
    D1 <--> E1
    D1 <--> E2
    D1 <--> E3
    D1 --> F1 --> F2
    D1 <--> F3
    G1 --> G2 --> D1
    G1 --> G3
    D1 --> H1
    D1 --> H2
    D1 --> H3
```

### 4.2 Data + Power Flow — End-to-End Physical-to-Cloud View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NODE["IoT DEVICE PHYSICAL DESIGN"]
        direction TB
        N1["Sensor Cluster"]
        N2["ADC + MCU"]
        N3["Radio Transceiver"]
        N4["Battery + PMU"]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3
        N4 -.powers.-> N1
        N4 -.powers.-> N2
        N4 -.powers.-> N3
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE / GATEWAY"]
        GW1["Smartphone or Edge Hub"]
        GW2["Local Storage / Aggregation"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD BACKEND"]
        CL1["Ingestion API"]
        CL2["Database"]
        CL3["Analytics / ML"]
        CL4["Doctor Dashboard / Alert Engine"]
    end

    N3 -->|Wireless Link| GW1
    GW1 --> GW2
    GW1 -->|MQTT / HTTPS| CL1
    CL1 --> CL2 --> CL3 --> CL4
    CL4 -->|Alert SMS / App| GW1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram for exams:** The upper half of the block diagram (sensor → processor → radio) is the **Physical Design**. The lower half (gateway → cloud) is the **Network / Communication Design**, *not* part of the physical design of the device. Examiners often award full marks only if this boundary is clearly labelled.

### 4.3 Energy/Power Distribution Within a Biomedical IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS["Li-Po 3.7 V 300 mAh"] --> REG["Buck Regulator 3V3"]
    REG --> MCU["MCU 25 mA peak"]
    REG --> RAD["Radio 80 mA peak"]
    REG --> SEN["Sensor Front-End 5 mA"]
    REG --> ACT["Actuator 50 mA peak"]
    MCU -.controls.-> SLEEP["Sleep Controller"]
    SLEEP -.gates power to.-> RAD
    SLEEP -.gates power to.-> ACT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Loses Marks)**
> 1. Drawing the *logical* (4-layer) IoT architecture when the question asks for *physical* design — **0 marks** for the diagram.
> 2. Confusing *sensor* with *actuator*; examiners specifically look for the distinction.
> 3. Omitting **power management** from the block diagram — a recurring 2-mark deduction.
> 4. Writing vague justifications such as "BLE is used because it is low power" — you *must* cite **range, data rate, and use-case** in a single sentence.
> 5. Forgetting units in numerical questions (e.g., mA vs. µA). The duty-cycle derivation in §3.2 is a common trap.

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List any three physical components of an IoT system and state their function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sensor** — converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, ECG voltage) into an electrical signal. *(1 mark)*
2. **Microcontroller / Processor** — runs the embedded firmware, performs ADC sampling, local processing, and controls the radio. *(1 mark)*
3. **Communication Module (Transceiver)** — transmits the acquired data wirelessly to a gateway or cloud using protocols such as BLE, Wi-Fi, or LoRa. *(1 mark)*

*(Optional 4th & 5th points for completeness: Memory — stores code/logs; Power Management Unit — supplies regulated voltage and monitors battery.)*

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator with one biomedical example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **sensor** *senses* a physical/biological quantity and converts it into an electrical signal. Example: a **PPG sensor** in a pulse oximeter that measures light absorption to compute SpO₂. *(1.5 marks)*
- An **actuator** *acts* upon the environment based on a control signal. Example: an **insulin pump stepper motor** that delivers a bolus dose to a diabetic patient. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) Draw the block diagram of the physical design of an IoT system and explain the function of each block. (7 marks)**
**(b) With a neat diagram, explain how a wearable ECG patch uses the physical-design principles to acquire, process, and transmit ECG data. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Block Diagram of the Physical Design of an IoT System (7 marks)**

```
 ┌────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌───────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
 │  SENSORS   │──▶│  PROCESSOR   │──▶│  MEMORY   │──▶│  COMM. MOD   │──▶ Cloud/Gateway
 └────────────┘   └──────────────┘   └───────────┘   └──────────────┘
        ▲                ▲                                    ▲
        │                └──── POWER MANAGEMENT ──────────────┘
        │
   ┌────────────┐
   │ ACTUATORS  │  (controlled by Processor)
   └────────────┘
```

**Functions of each block:**

1. **Sensors (1 mark):** Detect physical stimuli — temperature, pressure, heart rate, SpO₂, motion — and convert them into analog electrical signals. Examples: thermistor, ECG electrode, PPG photodiode.
2. **Processor / Microcontroller (1 mark):** The "brain" of the IoT device. Runs embedded firmware, controls sampling timing, performs local filtering/compression, and drives the radio. Examples: ARM Cortex-M0/M4, ESP32, nRF52840.
3. **Memory (1 mark):** Three tiers — Flash (firmware), SRAM (runtime), EEPROM/SD (data logging when network is unavailable).
4. **Communication Module (1 mark):** The transceiver (BLE/Wi-Fi/LoRa) and antenna that wirelessly transmit the data to the gateway or directly to the cloud.
5. **Power Management Unit (1 mark):** Includes battery, voltage regulator, and sleep controller. Provides stable 3.3 V supply and minimises idle current.
6. **Actuators (1 mark):** Perform physical actions — vibrating alarm, buzzer, Peltier cooler, drug-pump motor — based on processor commands.
7. **Connectivity diagram (1 mark):** Neat, labelled block diagram with arrows showing data and power flow.

**(b) Wearable ECG Patch — Physical-Design Realisation (7 marks)**

**Diagram (3 marks):**
```
┌─────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌─────────────┐
│ Ag/AgCl     │─▶│ Instrumentation│─▶│ 12-bit   │─▶│ Cortex-M0   │
│ Electrodes  │  │ Amplifier + HPF│  │ ADC      │  │ MCU         │
└─────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────┬──────┘
                                                        │
                                              ┌─────────▼────────┐
                                              │  BLE 5.0 Radio   │──▶ Smartphone App
                                              └─────────┬────────┘
                                                        │
                              ┌─────────────────────────▼──────┐
                              │ 3.7 V Li-Po + LDO + Sleep Ctrl│
                              └────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

- **Sensors (0.5):** Two Ag/AgCl electrodes on the chest detect the small differential ECG voltage (~1 mV).
- **Front-end (0.5):** An instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD8232) amplifies ~1000× and removes DC offset using a high-pass filter (~0.5 Hz).
- **Processor (0.5):** An ARM Cortex-M0 samples the ECG at 500 Hz, applies a 50 Hz notch filter for mains rejection, and detects R-peaks for heart rate.
- **Radio (0.5):** A BLE 5.0 SoC (e.g., nRF52832) transmits 250-byte packets every second to a paired smartphone.
- **Power (0.5):** A 3.7 V / 200 mAh Li-Po battery with an LDO regulator. The MCU spends 99 % of the time in sleep mode (current < 5 µA) — yielding an average current of ~0.3 mA and a battery life of ~28 days.
- **Enclosure (0.5):** A flexible silicone patch with IP67 rating, biocompatible adhesive, and a snap-on module — engineered for 7-day wear.
- **Use-case (0.5):** Enables continuous outpatient cardiac monitoring; the doctor views real-time ECG on a phone/tablet and receives alerts on arrhythmia.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a) Explain the role of the power management subsystem in an IoT device. A wearable IoT node is active for 8 ms and sleeps for 992 ms. Active current = 30 mA, sleep current = 4 µA. The battery is rated 250 mAh. Compute the average current and battery lifetime. (7 marks)**
**(b) Compare BLE, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, and LoRaWAN for use in a hospital-based biomedical IoT deployment. Justify your selection for two specific applications. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Power Management & Numerical Analysis (7 marks)**

**Role of Power Management (3 marks):**
- Provides a *stable, regulated* voltage (typically 3.3 V) to all subsystems from a battery source.
- Implements **duty-cycling** — waking the radio and processor only when needed — to minimise average current.
- **Supervises** battery health, performs **brown-out detection**, and gates power to inactive blocks through load switches.
- In *implantable* devices, low-power design is critical because battery replacement requires surgery.

**Numerical Computation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total period $T = 8 \text{ ms} + 992 \text{ ms} = 1000 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ s}$.

**Step 2:** Apply the duty-cycled average-current formula.

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{\text{act}} t_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{slp}} t_{\text{off}}}{T}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-3})(8 \times 10^{-3}) + (4 \times 10^{-6})(992 \times 10^{-3})}{1}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4} + 3.968 \times 10^{-6}}{1}$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 2.43968 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} \approx 244 \text{ µA}$$

**Step 3:** Battery lifetime.

$$t_{\text{life}} = \frac{250 \text{ mAh}}{0.244 \text{ mA}} \approx 1025 \text{ h} \approx 42.7 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion. *(0.5 mark)* Aggressive sleep-mode duty cycling extends operational life by ~3.4× compared to always-on operation (which would last only ~8.3 h).

**(b) Comparison of Wireless Protocols for Biomedical IoT (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | BLE 5.0 | Wi-Fi 6 | ZigBee | LoRaWAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 868 / 915 MHz |
| Range | ~10 m | ~50 m | ~100 m | 2–10 km |
| Data Rate | 1–2 Mbps | Up to 1 Gbps | 250 kbps | 0.3–50 kbps |
| Tx Current | 5–15 mA | 100–300 mA | 20–30 mA | 50–100 mA |
| Top Use | Wearables | Bedside monitors | Body-area network | Rural telemonitoring |

**Two Justified Applications (2 + 2 = 4 marks):**

1. **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) for a diabetic patient → BLE 5.0.** *Justification:* The CGM is body-worn, needs to pair with a smartphone, transmits only a few kilobytes per day (one glucose reading every 5 minutes), and runs on a coin cell for 14 days. BLE's ultra-low current draw (5–15 mA peak, < 1 µA sleep) and short range are ideal; Wi-Fi would drain the battery in < 2 days.

2. **Rural ASHA-Worker Remote Heart-Failure Monitor → LoRaWAN.** *Justification:* The patient lives in a village 5 km from the nearest Primary Health Centre. Wi-Fi/ZigBee have insufficient range; cellular NB-IoT has subscription cost. LoRaWAN provides 5–10 km line-of-sight coverage, 0.3 kbps is enough for a daily 1-minute ECG strip, and battery lasts > 1 year.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Physical Design of IoT**

- **Five physical subsystems →** Sensor, Processor, Memory, Communication, Power — memorise as **S-P-M-C-P**.
- **Sensor ≠ Actuator** — sensors *sense* (input), actuators *act* (output). Give one biomedical example of each in every answer.
- **Block diagram must include Power Management** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if it is missing.
- **Common MCUs:** 8-bit ATmega → 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4 → SoC with integrated radio (ESP32, nRF52).
- **Common voltages:** battery **3.7 V** (Li-Ion), regulated rail **3.3 V**.
- **ISM bands:** **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi/BLE/ZigBee), **868/915 MHz** (LoRa), **433 MHz** (industrial Sub-GHz).
- **Key formula 1 — ADC LSB:** $\Delta V = V_{\text{FS}} / (2^{n}-1)$ — 12-bit ADC at 3.3 V → **~0.806 mV / LSB**.
- **Key formula 2 — Average current (duty-cycled):** $I_{\text{avg}} = (I_{\text{act}} t_{\text{on}} + I_{\text{slp}} t_{\text{off}}) / T$.
- **Key formula 3 — Battery life:** $t_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$; ECG 500 Hz, EEG 250 Hz, body-temp 1 Hz.
- **Protocol selection** is always a *Power–Range–Bandwidth* trade-off — there is no universally "best" wireless protocol.
- **Distinguish** *Physical Design* (hardware anatomy) from *Logical Design* (4-layer architecture) — examiners test this in the 3-mark and 7-mark parts.
- **Wearable ECG patch** is the *default biomedical example*; know its block diagram end-to-end.
- **Regulatory** context for biomedical IoT: **IEC 60601**, **IP67** for water resistance, biocompatible enclosures.
- **Avoid** writing "BLE is low power" without numbers — cite a peak current and a sleep current to earn full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logical Design of IoT – IoT Functional Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logical Design of IoT – IoT Functional Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logical Design of IoT** refers to the high-level, abstract architectural representation of an Internet of Things (IoT) system that describes *what* functional components are needed, *how* they interact, and *what* each component is supposed to do — without being tied to any specific hardware manufacturer, protocol stack, or product implementation. It is a **black-box behavioral view** as opposed to a *physical (circuit-level) design*.

According to the KTU 2024 OEBMT831 syllabus, the **IoT Functional Blocks** are the standardized logical sub-systems (modules) that every IoT device, gateway, or system can be decomposed into. These blocks are *device-agnostic* and *application-agnostic* — they form a universal vocabulary for describing any IoT solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – OEBMT831, Module 1):**
> Students must be able to (i) enumerate the **functional blocks** of an IoT system, (ii) draw the **logical block diagram**, (iii) describe the **role of each block**, and (iv) differentiate between **logical design and physical design**.

The standard functional blocks recognized globally (and adopted by KTU) are:

| # | Functional Block | One-Line Purpose |
|---|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Sensing / Actuation Block** | Interfaces with the physical world (captures or alters phenomena). |
| 2 | **Communication / Network Block** | Carries data to/from the device using wired/wireless links. |
| 3 | **Processing / Computation Block** | Runs logic, makes local decisions, executes IoT algorithms. |
| 4 | **Storage / Memory Block** | Retains data, code, configuration, and logs locally. |
| 5 | **Power / Energy Block** | Supplies and manages electrical energy to all sub-blocks. |
| 6 | **Security / Privacy Block** | Enforces authentication, encryption, and tamper resistance. |
| 7 | **Identification / Addressing Block** | Provides unique device IDs, IP/MAC, and naming. |
| 8 | **Interface / Application Block** | Presents data to user / cloud and accepts commands. |
| 9 | **Localization Block** (advanced) | Determines geographic position (GPS, BLE beacons, Wi-Fi triangulation). |
| 10 | **Management / Configuration Block** | Handles firmware updates, provisioning, and diagnostics. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "IoT Smartwatch" Metaphor

Imagine an IoT device as a **human body working in a hospital ICU**:

- 👁️ **Sensors** = *eyes, ears, and skin* — they *sense* temperature, pulse, pressure.
- 🤚 **Actuators** = *hands* — they *act* (e.g., inject insulin via a pump).
- 🧠 **Processor** = *brain (CPU)* — it *thinks*, runs the BP/heart-rate algorithm.
- 💾 **Storage** = *memory* — it *remembers* past readings.
- 📡 **Communication** = *mouth and ears (radio)* — it *talks* to the nurse station (cloud).
- 🔋 **Power** = *heart* — it *pumps energy* to all organs.
- 🔐 **Security** = *immune system* — it *protects* against viruses/tampering.
- 🆔 **Identification** = *name-tag / Aadhaar ID* — uniquely identifies the patient.
- 🖥️ **Interface** = *monitor display* — shows the doctor what is happening.
- 📍 **Localization** = *room number / GPS tracker* — knows *where* the patient is.
- ⚙️ **Management** = *ward-boy who updates charts* — reconfigures & updates firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Every IoT gadget — be it a wearable ECG patch, a smart fridge, or a hospital bed sensor — can be *logically* broken down into this same set of 8–10 functional blocks. The *physical* implementation may differ (an Arduino vs. a Raspberry Pi vs. an ASIC), but the *logical architecture* is invariant.

---

## 1.3 Logical Design vs. Physical Design

| Aspect | Logical Design | Physical Design |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Focus | **What** the system does | **How** it is built (chips, pins, voltages) |
| Abstraction | High-level block diagram | Circuit schematic / PCB layout |
| Audience | System architects, software devs | Hardware engineers, manufacturers |
| Changes when | Requirements change | Components, vendors, or cost change |
| KTU weightage | **Module 1 (this topic)** | Module 2 / Lab |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the order of blocks → "S-C-P-S²-I-L-M-A":**
> **S**ensing → **C**ommunication → **P**rocessing → **S**ecurity → **S**torage → **I**dentification → **L**ocalization → **M**anagement → **A**pplication/Interface.

# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Each Functional Block

### 🔹 A) Sensing / Actuation Block
- **Sensors** convert physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, SpO₂, ECG voltage) into electrical signals.
- **Actuators** do the reverse: convert electrical commands into physical action (motor, valve, LED, buzzer).
- Interface: ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) for sensors; DAC / driver for actuators.
- Example: MAX30102 (PPG sensor) + SG90 servo (actuator in a ventilator prototype).

### 🔹 B) Communication / Network Block
- Responsible for **data transmission** between the IoT device, gateway, and cloud.
- Sub-categorized by range:

$$ \text{Range} = \begin{cases} \text{PAN / LAN} & \text{BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, NFC} \\ \text{LPWAN} & \text{LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT} \\ \text{WAN} & \text{4G/5G, Satellite} \end{cases} $$

- Implements the **communication protocol stack** (Physical → MAC → Network → Transport → Application).

### 🔹 C) Processing / Computation Block
- The "brain" — typically a **microcontroller (MCU)** like ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, STM32, or a **microprocessor (MPU)** like Raspberry Pi for edge-computing.
- Runs the IoT firmware, edge analytics, and decision logic.
- Key metrics: **clock speed (MHz)**, **core count**, **power consumption (mW)**, **operating voltage (3.3 V / 5 V)**.

### 🔹 D) Storage / Memory Block
- Two types:
  - **Volatile (RAM):** for runtime data, stack, heap.
  - **Non-Volatile (Flash / EEPROM / SD card):** for firmware, logs, configuration.
- Trade-off: faster memory = more cost + more power.

$$ \text{Useful life of Flash} \propto \frac{1}{P/E_{\text{cycles}}} $$

where $P/E_{\text{cycles}}$ is the program/erase cycle count (typically **100 000 cycles** for MLC NAND).

### 🔹 E) Power / Energy Block
- Sources: battery (Li-ion 3.7 V), energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric, RF), mains adapter, USB.
- Critical metric: **battery life (hours/days)**:

$$ T_{\text{life}} \;[\text{hours}] \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{bat}} \;[\text{mAh}]}{I_{\text{avg}} \;[\text{mA}]} $$

- A wearable ECG patch must achieve **$T_{\text{life}} \geq 168\ \text{h}$** (one week) for clinical acceptance.

### 🔹 F) Security / Privacy Block
- Implements **CIA triad**: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability.
- Hardware roots-of-trust: TPM, secure elements (ATECC608B).
- Software: AES-128/256, TLS 1.3, mutual authentication.

### 🔹 G) Identification / Addressing Block
- Provides unique identity — MAC-48, EUI-64, IPv6 address, or EPC (RFID).
- For biomedical IoT, the **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** system is mandated by the **US FDA** and the **EU MDR**.

### 🔹 H) Localization Block
- Technologies: **GPS** (5–10 m outdoor), **BLE beacons** (1–3 m indoor), **Wi-Fi fingerprinting** (3–5 m), **UWB** (10–30 cm).
- For a hospital IoT asset tracker, sub-meter accuracy is required → UWB or BLE 5.1 AoA.

### 🔹 I) Management / Configuration Block
- Handles **firmware OTA (Over-The-Air) updates**, remote provisioning, telemetry.
- Standards: LwM2M (OMA SpecWorks), SUIT (IETF).

### 🔹 J) Interface / Application Block
- The user-facing layer — mobile app, dashboard, REST API, MQTT topic.
- Accepts commands (write) and presents data (read).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates *every* standard equation, constant, and metric that can be asked in a KTU ESE for this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Real-World Context |
|---|---------|------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Battery life | $T_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$ | hours | Wearable design |
| 2 | Flash endurance | $P/E_{\text{cycles}}$ | **100 k cycles** (MLC), **10 k** (TLC) | Edge device logging |
| 3 | Data rate (Shannon) | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/second | Throughput planning |
| 4 | Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | Hz | ECG = 250–500 Hz |
| 5 | Power budget | $P_{\text{total}} = \sum P_{\text{block}_i}$ | mW / W | Low-power design |
| 6 | Duty cycle (energy save) | $D = T_{\text{on}} / T_{\text{period}}$ | dimensionless (%) | BLE beacons |
| 7 | Latency budget | $L_{\text{end-to-end}} = \sum L_i$ | ms | Telemedicine |
| 8 | Range (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ | dBm | Wireless link budget |
| 9 | Device density | $\rho = N / A$ | devices/m² | Hospital ward |
| 10 | Security level | AES key length | **128 / 192 / 256 bits** | HIPAA / GDPR |

> **Reminder — avoid `\|` inside any markdown table cell.** All absolute values are shown with `\vert` instead.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **biomedical IoT deployment** (e.g., a smart ICU), each functional block is non-negotiable:

- **Sensing**: SpO₂, ECG, body temperature, airflow, capnography.
- **Actuation**: Ventilator valve, IV pump motor, alarm buzzer.
- **Processing**: Real-time arrhythmia detection on edge.
- **Communication**: BLE → gateway → MQTT broker → cloud EMR.
- **Power**: Battery backup for **$\geq 4$ hours** during power failure (hospital code requirement).
- **Security**: AES-128 encryption to satisfy **HIPAA** / **DISHA (India, 2018)**.
- **Identification**: UDI barcode on every connected device.
- **Localization**: BLE-based asset tracking for crash-cart.
- **Management**: OTA updates for new arrhythmia detection algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The logical design is *implementation-independent* — the *same* block diagram describes both a ₹500 Arduino-based health monitor *and* a ₹5 lakh FDA-certified wearable Holter. This is why KTU tests the *logical* view first.

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — End-to-End Latency of a Biomedical IoT Pipeline

> **Problem Setup:** A wearable ECG patch transmits 1 sample packet to the cloud. Estimate the **total end-to-end latency** $L_{\text{e2e}}$ through all 6 functional blocks involved in the data path.

### Step 1 — Identify the latency contribution of each block

$$ L_{\text{e2e}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{ADC}} + L_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{tx}} + L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{cloud}} $$

**Conversion logic:** Latency is *additive* for a serial data path (one packet traverses blocks sequentially). For parallel paths, the maximum dominates — but here we assume a single-packet, single-path scenario.

### Step 2 — Substitute standard numerical values

| Block | Symbol | Typical Value | Justification |
|-------|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Sensor settling | $L_{\text{sense}}$ | **5 ms** | ECG electrode RC time constant |
| ADC conversion | $L_{\text{ADC}}$ | **0.1 ms** | 12-bit SAR @ 1 MSPS |
| MCU processing | $L_{\text{proc}}$ | **2 ms** | Filtering + feature extraction |
| Radio TX | $L_{\text{tx}}$ | **3 ms** | BLE 4.2 connection interval |
| Network hop | $L_{\text{net}}$ | **20 ms** | Wi-Fi + cloud round-trip |
| Cloud response | $L_{\text{cloud}}$ | **50 ms** | TLS handshake + processing |

### Step 3 — Perform the arithmetic

$$ \begin{aligned}
L_{\text{e2e}} &= L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{ADC}} + L_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{tx}} + L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{cloud}} \\[4pt]
&= 5\ \text{ms} + 0.1\ \text{ms} + 2\ \text{ms} + 3\ \text{ms} + 20\ \text{ms} + 50\ \text{ms} \\[4pt]
&= 80.1\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned} $$

**Conversion logic:** Direct sum of homogeneous quantities (all in milliseconds). Result remains in **ms**.

### Step 4 — Verify against clinical requirement

> For a real-time arrhythmia alarm, the standard is **$L_{\text{e2e}} < 300\ \text{ms}$** (per IEC 60601-1-8 medical alarm guidelines).

$$ 80.1\ \text{ms} \;\ll\; 300\ \text{ms} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{System meets clinical SLA.} \;\checkmark $$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Power Budget of a Battery-Operated IoT Sensor Node

### Step 1 — Enumerate the power consumption of each functional block

$$ P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{MCU}} + P_{\text{radio}} + P_{\text{sensor}} + P_{\text{storage}} + P_{\text{misc}} $$

### Step 2 — Substitute typical values for a BLE-based ECG patch

| Block | Symbol | Active Power | Sleep Power | Duty Cycle |
|-------|--------|--------------|-------------|------------|
| MCU (nRF52840) | $P_{\text{MCU}}$ | **12 mW** | 0.003 mW | 10 % |
| Radio (BLE 5) | $P_{\text{radio}}$ | **30 mW** | 0.001 mW | 5 % |
| Sensor (AD8232) | $P_{\text{sensor}}$ | **8 mW** | 0.010 mW | 100 % |
| Storage (Flash) | $P_{\text{storage}}$ | **15 mW** | 0.001 mW | 1 % |
| Misc (LEDs, etc.) | $P_{\text{misc}}$ | **5 mW** | 0 mW | 5 % |

### Step 3 — Compute the average power using the duty-cycle model

$$ P_{\text{avg}} = \sum_i D_i \cdot P_{\text{active},i} + (1 - D_i) \cdot P_{\text{sleep},i} $$

**Conversion logic:** Each block spends fraction $D_i$ of time *active* and $(1 - D_i)$ *sleeping*. Total average is the weighted sum.

### Step 4 — Compute each term

$$ \begin{aligned}
P_{\text{MCU,avg}} &= 0.10 \times 12 + 0.90 \times 0.003 = 1.2027\ \text{mW} \\
P_{\text{radio,avg}} &= 0.05 \times 30 + 0.95 \times 0.001 = 1.5009\ \text{mW} \\
P_{\text{sensor,avg}} &= 1.00 \times 8 + 0.00 \times 0.010 = 8.0000\ \text{mW} \\
P_{\text{storage,avg}} &= 0.01 \times 15 + 0.99 \times 0.001 = 0.1510\ \text{mW} \\
P_{\text{misc,avg}} &= 0.05 \times 5 + 0.95 \times 0 = 0.2500\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned} $$

### Step 5 — Sum to obtain $P_{\text{total,avg}}$

$$ \begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total,avg}} &= 1.2027 + 1.5009 + 8.0000 + 0.1510 + 0.2500 \\
&= 11.1046\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned} $$

### Step 6 — Estimate battery life on a 400 mAh @ 3.7 V Li-Po cell

$$ \begin{aligned}
E_{\text{bat}} &= V_{\text{bat}} \times C_{\text{bat}} = 3.7\ \text{V} \times 400\ \text{mAh} = 1480\ \text{mWh} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{life}} &= \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{total,avg}}} = \frac{1480\ \text{mWh}}{11.1046\ \text{mW}} \approx 133.3\ \text{hours} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.55\ \text{days}
\end{aligned} $$

**Conversion logic:** Battery energy is the product of nominal voltage and charge capacity. Life is energy divided by average power drain — a direct application of $E = P \cdot t$.

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** If we doubled the radio duty cycle (e.g., always-on BLE), the radio term alone would jump to 30 mW and life would drop to ~75 h. This shows *why* sleep-mode design is critical.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Communication Block

| Criterion | BLE 5.0 | ZigBee | Wi-Fi | LoRa |
|-----------|---------|--------|-------|------|
| Range | $\sim 50$ m | $\sim 100$ m | $\sim 100$ m | $\sim 10$ km |
| Data rate | **2 Mbps** | 250 kbps | 54 Mbps | 50 kbps |
| Power | **Low** | Low | High | **Very Low** |
| Best for | Wearables | Home automation | High-throughput | Field / rural |
| KTU weightage | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a *biomedical IoT wearable* in a hospital ward, **BLE** is the KTU-preferred answer because of low power + adequate throughput + smartphone compatibility.

# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master IoT Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **complete logical design of an IoT system** with the 8 standard functional blocks and their canonical data/energy flows.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHY[PHYSICAL WORLD]
        env[(Environment Phenomenon)]
    end

    subgraph DEV[IoT DEVICE LOGICAL DESIGN]
        sa[SENSING AND ACTUATION BLOCK]
        proc[PROCESSING AND COMPUTATION BLOCK]
        sto[STORAGE AND MEMORY BLOCK]
        pwr[POWER AND ENERGY BLOCK]
        sec[SECURITY AND PRIVACY BLOCK]
        idn[IDENTIFICATION AND ADDRESSING BLOCK]
        loc[LOCALIZATION BLOCK]
        mgr[MANAGEMENT AND CONFIGURATION BLOCK]
        com[COMMUNICATION AND NETWORK BLOCK]
    end

    subgraph EXT[EXTERNAL ENTITIES]
        gw[IoT GATEWAY]
        cloud[(CLOUD AND APPLICATION LAYER)]
        user[END USER AND INTERFACE]
    end

    env -->|Physical signal| sa
    sa -->|Raw electrical data| proc
    proc -->|Reads or Writes| sto
    proc -->|Encrypts or Decrypts| sec
    proc -->|Tagged with ID| idn
    proc -->|Location stamped| loc
    sa -->|Actuation command| env
    pwr -.->|DC power| sa
    pwr -.->|DC power| proc
    pwr -.->|DC power| com
    pwr -.->|DC power| sto
    pwr -.->|DC power| sec
    pwr -.->|DC power| idn
    pwr -.->|DC power| loc
    pwr -.->|DC power| mgr
    mgr -.->|OTA and config| proc
    mgr -.->|OTA and config| com
    proc -->|Payload packet| com
    com -->|Wireless or Wired| gw
    gw -->|Internet| cloud
    cloud -->|Dashboard or API| user
    user -->|Command| cloud
    cloud -->|Downlink| gw
    gw -->|Downlink| com
    com -->|Command| proc
```

### Diagram Reading Guide

- **Solid arrows (`-->`)** = data flow (packets, sensor readings, commands).
- **Dotted arrows (`-.->`)** = energy or control flow (power rails, OTA updates).
- **Three sub-graphs** = (1) physical world, (2) device-internal logical blocks, (3) external infrastructure.
- The **Power block** touches *every* other block — it is the only block that supplies *all* others.
- The **Security block** wraps the **Processing block** — all sensitive operations route through it.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Biomedical Wearable View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIO SIGNAL FROM BODY] --> B1[ECG SENSOR]
    A --> B2[SpO2 SENSOR]
    A --> B3[TEMPERATURE SENSOR]

    B1 --> C[ADC MULTIPLEXER]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C

    C --> D[MCU PROCESSOR]
    D <--> E[FLASH MEMORY]
    D <--> F[SECURE ELEMENT]
    D --> G[BLE RADIO]
    G --> H[SMARTPHONE GATEWAY]
    H --> I[CLOUD EMR SERVER]
    I --> J[DOCTOR DASHBOARD]

    K[LITHIUM BATTERY] -.-> D
    K -.-> G
    K -.-> B1
    K -.-> B2
    K -.-> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram is helpful:** It shows how the *abstract* functional blocks of Section 1.1 are *instantiated* in a real biomedical product. The KTU examiner loves to ask: *"Map the functional blocks to the components of a wearable ECG patch."*

---

## 4.3 Functional-Block Interaction Matrix

| Block ↓ Sends / Receives → | Sensor | Processor | Storage | Security | Radio | Power | App |
|----------------------------|:------:|:---------:|:-------:|:--------:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| **Sensor**                 | —      | ✔         |         |          |       | ⇐     |     |
| **Processor**              | ✔      | —         | ↔       | ↔        | ➞     | ⇐     |     |
| **Storage**                |        | ↔         | —       |          |       | ⇐     |     |
| **Security**               |        | ↔         |         | —        | ↔     | ⇐     |     |
| **Radio**                  |        | ⇐         |         | ↔        | —     | ⇐     | ↔   |
| **Power**                  | ➞      | ➞         | ➞       | ➞        | ➞     | —     |     |
| **App / UI**               |        |           |         |          | ↔     |       | —   |

- **✔** = primary data flow
- **↔** = bidirectional exchange
- **➞** = one-way energy supply
- **⇐** = one-way energy receipt

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "IoT Functional Block". List any FOUR standard functional blocks of an IoT system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **IoT Functional Block** is a *logical, implementation-independent sub-system* that performs a specific role inside an IoT device or system. Logical design uses these blocks to describe the architecture at a high level.

Any four of the following (½ mark each for naming + ½ mark for correct role):

1. **Sensing/Actuation Block** — captures physical phenomena (e.g., temperature, ECG) or actuates physical change (e.g., motor).
2. **Communication Block** — transmits/receives data via wireless/wired links (BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa).
3. **Processing Block** — executes logic, decision algorithms, and edge analytics using an MCU/MPU.
4. **Power/Energy Block** — supplies regulated DC power to all other blocks (battery, mains, harvesting).
5. **Storage Block** — retains firmware, configuration, and buffered data (Flash, EEPROM).
6. **Security/Privacy Block** — enforces encryption, authentication, and tamper detection.
7. **Identification Block** — assigns unique IDs (MAC, IP, EPC, UDI).
8. **Interface/Application Block** — presents data to user and accepts commands (mobile app, dashboard).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4 blocks with their role.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
**Differentiate between Logical Design and Physical Design of an IoT system. Give ONE example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Logical Design | Physical Design |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Definition | High-level architectural view of *what* blocks exist and how they interact. | Hardware-level view of *how* the blocks are realized using chips, circuits, and PCBs. |
| Abstraction | Block diagrams, data-flow graphs | Circuit schematics, Gerber files |
| Example | *"The wearable has a Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power, and Security block."* | *"Sensing is implemented using AD8232 ECG front-end; Processing is implemented using Nordic nRF52840 BLE SoC; power is a 3.7 V 400 mAh Li-Po with TPS63001 buck-boost regulator."* |

*(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for the example pair.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE 14-Mark Module Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** *Answer ONE full question from the module. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Sub-parts may have internal choice.*

### ❓ QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **complete logical block diagram of a generic IoT system**, identifying the **eight standard functional blocks** and the **direction of data and energy flow** between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **wearable ECG monitoring patch** used in a hospital:
  - (i) **Map** the eight functional blocks to specific hardware components. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) Compute the **battery life** for a 400 mAh, 3.7 V Li-Po cell, given the average current draw is **12 mA**. State whether the device meets the **clinical 7-day wear-time requirement**. **(4 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Diagram (4 marks):**
Draw the block diagram exactly as in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The examiner awards:
- 1 mark each for correctly drawing **any 6 of the 8** standard blocks.
- 1 mark for correct **arrow directions** (data vs. power).
- 1 mark for **labelling** each block clearly.

**Step 2 — Explanation (3 marks — ½ mark per block, max 3 blocks explained in depth):**
- **Sensing/Actuation**: Converts physical signals (ECG, SpO₂) into electrical signals using electrodes/optical sensors. Uses ADC for digitization.
- **Processing**: Runs the embedded firmware. In an IoT device, it executes the edge-analytics algorithm (e.g., Pan-Tompkins QRS detector) and decides when to transmit.
- **Communication**: Encodes digital data into radio frames (BLE advertisements) and transmits via the antenna. Handles the protocol stack (PHY → APP).
- **Power**: A Li-Po battery coupled to a DC-DC regulator. Must maintain $V_{\text{out}} = 3.3\ \text{V} \pm 5\%$ for the MCU.
- **Security**: AES-128 hardware accelerator on the SoC; secure boot from immutable ROM; TLS handshake with cloud.
- **Storage**: 1 MB internal Flash for firmware + 64 KB RAM for runtime stack.
- **Identification**: 48-bit BLE MAC + 128-bit UUID service.
- **Application/Interface**: Cloud dashboard + mobile app (Android/iOS) using REST/MQTT.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]

**(b-i) Hardware Mapping (3 marks):**

| Functional Block | Hardware Component (example) |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| Sensing | AD8232 ECG front-end + 3 electrodes |
| Processing | Nordic nRF52840 (ARM Cortex-M4) |
| Storage | 1 MB internal Flash + 256 KB RAM |
| Communication | nRF52840 integrated BLE 5 radio |
| Power | 400 mAh Li-Po + TI BQ25185 PMIC |
| Security | Onboard AES-128 + secure boot ROM |
| Identification | BLE MAC address (factory-programmed) |
| Interface | REST API to cloud + Android app |

**(b-ii) Battery-Life Calculation (4 marks):**

$$ \begin{aligned}
C_{\text{bat}} &= 400\ \text{mAh}, \quad V_{\text{bat}} = 3.7\ \text{V} \\
I_{\text{avg}} &= 12\ \text{mA} \\
T_{\text{life}} &= \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{400\ \text{mAh}}{12\ \text{mA}} = 33.33\ \text{hours} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{life}} &= 33.33\ \text{h} \approx 1.39\ \text{days}
\end{aligned} $$

**Clinical 7-day requirement:** $T_{\text{required}} = 7 \times 24 = 168\ \text{hours}$

$$ 33.33\ \text{h} \;\ll\; 168\ \text{h} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \textbf{Does NOT meet the 7-day requirement.} $$

**Recommendation (1 mark):** Reduce $I_{\text{avg}}$ to $C_{\text{bat}} / T_{\text{req}} = 400 / 168 = 2.38\ \text{mA}$ by aggressive sleep-mode design (BLE duty cycle 1 %, MCU sleep between ADC samples).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the role of the following **five functional blocks** in an IoT system: (i) Sensing/Actuation, (ii) Communication, (iii) Processing, (iv) Power, (v) Security. For each block, give **one biomedical example**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat **logical block diagram**, describe how a **smart insulin pump** (used by Type-1 diabetic patients) can be decomposed into IoT functional blocks. Identify at least **six blocks** and explain the data flow from glucose sensing to insulin delivery. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]

**Allocate: 1.4 marks per block (rounded), split as 0.7 marks role + 0.7 marks example.**

| Block | Role (½ mark) | Biomedical Example (½ mark) |
|-------|---------------|------------------------------|
| (i) Sensing/Actuation | Converts physical/chemical signals to electrical (and vice versa) | **Example:** Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) sensor measures interstitial glucose; insulin pump motor acts as actuator. |
| (ii) Communication | Handles data exchange via wireless/wired protocols | **Example:** BLE radio transmits glucose readings from CGM to smartphone. |
| (iii) Processing | Executes decision logic and edge analytics | **Example:** Embedded algorithm on the pump computes insulin dose = $f(\text{CGM}, \text{meal}, \text{activity})$. |
| (iv) Power | Supplies and manages energy to all sub-blocks | **Example:** Rechargeable Li-Po with energy-harvesting from body heat (low-power domain). |
| (v) Security | Ensures confidentiality, integrity, authentication | **Example:** AES-128 encryption to protect patient data per **HIPAA** / **DISHA** compliance. |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Interstitial Glucose] --> B[SENSOR CGM]
    B --> C[ADC]
    C --> D[MCU PROCESSOR]
    D <--> E[FLASH MEMORY]
    D --> F[BLE RADIO]
    D --> G[SECURE ELEMENT]
    F --> H[SMARTPHONE APP]
    H --> I[CLOUD EMR]
    D --> J[MOTOR DRIVER]
    J --> K[INSULIN PUMP ACTUATOR]
    K --> L[Subcutaneous Delivery]
    M[LITHIUM BATTERY] -.-> D
    M -.-> B
    M -.-> F
    M -.-> J
```

**Step 2 — Data Flow Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **CGM sensor** (e.g., Dexcom G6) reads interstitial glucose every 5 min — *Sensing block*.
2. **ADC** digitizes the analog signal — *part of Sensing/Processing bridge*.
3. **MCU** runs a Model-Predictive-Control (MPC) algorithm: $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt$ where $e(t) = G_{\text{target}} - G_{\text{measured}}$ — *Processing block*.
4. Calculated dose is sent to **motor driver** that turns the lead-screw of the pump — *Actuation block*.
5. **BLE radio** simultaneously uploads reading to the patient's smartphone and **cloud EMR** for clinician review — *Communication + Application blocks*.
6. **Secure element** (e.g., Maxim MAXQ1062) signs every packet with a private key — *Security block*.
7. **Li-Po battery** with PMIC powers everything for 72 h between charges — *Power block*.
8. **Flash memory** stores 30 days of dose history — *Storage block*.

**Conversion logic (1 mark):** Emphasize the **closed-loop control** — the system is essentially a *cyber-physical feedback loop* where sensing → processing → actuation is cycled every 5 minutes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "Functional Block" with "Physical Component"** — e.g., writing *"nRF52840"* instead of *"Processing Block"*. Always answer at the *logical* level first.
> 2. **Forgetting the Power block** — the power block is often missed because it is "invisible" in flow diagrams, but it supplies *every* other block.
> 3. **Mixing up data flow and power flow** — use *solid* arrows for data, *dotted* arrows for power. Examiners deduct ½ mark for ambiguity.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — always write "mAh", "mA", "hours", "ms". An answer like *"33.33"* without units is treated as wrong.
> 5. **Writing only 4–5 blocks instead of 8** — KTU's expected answer for "draw the logical block diagram" is *minimum 6–8* blocks. List all that apply.
> 6. **Ignoring security** — for biomedical IoT, security is *not optional*; mentioning AES/TLS/HIPAA always earns a +1 bonus mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision card — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Logical Design** = high-level, *what* the system does (not *how*).
- [x] **Physical Design** = low-level, *how* the system is built (chips, circuits, PCBs).
- [x] **8–10 standard functional blocks:** Sensing, Actuation, Processing, Storage, Power, Communication, Security, Identification, Localization, Management, Application/Interface.
- [x] **Data flow** uses *solid* arrows; **Power flow** uses *dotted* arrows.
- [x] **Power block** supplies **EVERY** other block — never omit it.
- [x] **Security block** wraps the **Processing block** — non-negotiable in medical IoT.
- [x] **Battery life formula:** $T_{\text{life}} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$.
- [x] **Energy:** $E_{\text{bat}} = V_{\text{bat}} \times C_{\text{bat}}$ (in mWh).
- [x] **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ — ECG needs **250–500 Hz**.
- [x] **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- [x] **BLE 5** is the KTU-preferred radio for biomedical wearables.
- [x] **AES-128 / 256** is the standard encryption for medical IoT.
- [x] **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** is mandatory per FDA & EU MDR.
- [x] **Standards to mention** for bonus marks: **HIPAA, DISHA, IEC 60601-1-8, IEEE 11073** (medical device communication).
- [x] **Mnemonic:** **"S-C-P-S²-I-L-M-A"** = Sensing, Communication, Processing, Security, Storage, Identification, Localization, Management, Application.
- [x] **End-to-end latency target** for medical alarms: **< 300 ms**.

> **All the best for your KTU ESE — read the question carefully, list the blocks before drawing, and ALWAYS mention units! 🎯**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Communication Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Communication Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IoT Communication Models** are standardized architectural reference patterns that define *how data flows* between the various heterogeneous entities (sensors, actuators, gateways, cloud servers, and end-user applications) that constitute an Internet of Things ecosystem. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, these models abstract the underlying transport, session, and application-layer protocols (such as **MQTT**, **CoAP**, **HTTP**, **AMQP**, and **DDS**) into reusable structural templates that designers can deploy across diverse verticals — from biomedical telemetry to industrial automation.

The **International Telecommunication Union (ITU-T Y.4000/Y.2060** recommendation) recognizes **four primary communication models** and **five foundational models** as the canonical classification. These are listed in **bold** below for high-priority recall:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical IoT Communication Models (ITU-T Y.2060)**
> 1. **Device-to-Device (D2D) Communication Model**
> 2. **Device-to-Cloud (D2C) Communication Model**
> 3. **Device-to-Gateway (D2G) Communication Model**
> 4. **Back-End Data-Sharing (BaaS) Communication Model**
>
> Extended models often used: **Publish/Subscribe**, **Request/Response**, **Push/Pull**, **Exclusive Pairing**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital ICU Analogy

Think of an **IoT system as a fully networked hospital ICU**:

- **Patients with biomedical sensors** = IoT *Devices* (e.g., SpO₂, ECG, blood-pressure monitors).
- **Bedside monitors displaying vitals** = *Local Gateway / Sink Node*.
- **The Hospital Central Monitoring Server** = The *Cloud Platform*.
- **Doctors' smartphones, ICU dashboards, family-notification apps** = *End-User Applications*.

In this analogy, the **Communication Model** defines *how the patient’s heartbeat data reaches the doctor's screen*:

- Sometimes the bedside monitor talks **directly to the doctor's phone** (D2D over BLE).
- Sometimes it uploads to the **central server** and the doctor reads it from there (D2C).
- Sometimes a **local nurse station** consolidates data from many patients before forwarding (D2G).
- Sometimes the central server **redistributes** the ECG to a cardiologist, a research database, and an AI analytics engine simultaneously (Back-End Data Sharing).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are Communication Models Important?**
> They decouple *application logic* from *transport mechanics*, allowing biomedical engineers to design **scalable, interoperable, and secure** patient-monitoring systems without redesigning the data-flow architecture for every new sensor.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following standard engineering metrics govern the choice of communication model in biomedical IoT:

- **Latency (L):** measured in **milliseconds (ms)**; critical threshold for life-saving alarms is **< 100 ms**.
- **Bandwidth (B):** measured in **kilobits per second (kbps)**; ECG stream ≈ **72 kbps** uncompressed.
- **Power Consumption (P):** measured in **milliwatts (mW)**; BLE = **~10 mW**, Wi-Fi = **~800 mW**.
- **Reliability (R):** packet delivery ratio ≥ **99.9 %** for medical-grade IoT (per **ISO/IEEE 11073**).
- **Range (d):** Body-Area Network = **0–3 m**, Personal Area Network = **< 10 m**, Local Area Network = **< 100 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Communication Models — Logical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Device-to-Device (D2D) Model
- **What:** Two or more IoT devices communicate *directly* without an intermediary application server.
- **Why used:** Eliminates round-trip through the cloud → ultra-low latency.
- **How it works:** Devices pair using short-range protocols — **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)**, **Zigbee**, **Z-Wave**, or **Wi-Fi Direct**.
- **Biomedical example:** A wearable ECG patch streams data **directly** to a bedside tablet.
- **Limitation:** No persistent storage; both devices must be **co-located and online** simultaneously.

### 2.1.2 Device-to-Cloud (D2C) Model
- **What:** Devices connect *directly* to a public or private cloud server over the **Internet** (using TCP/IP, HTTP, MQTT, or CoAP).
- **Why used:** Provides **global accessibility**, long-term storage, and Big-Data analytics.
- **How it works:** Device → Wi-Fi/4G/5G modem → Internet → Cloud REST API / MQTT Broker.
- **Biomedical example:** A remote glucometer pushes readings to a hospital **AWS IoT Core** server; an endocrinologist views them on a dashboard.
- **Limitation:** High power draw; **single point of failure** at the cloud.

### 2.1.3 Device-to-Gateway (D2G) Model
- **What:** Devices first talk to an **intermediate gateway** (a smartphone, edge router, or medical hub), which then forwards data to the cloud.
- **Why used:** Bridges **non-IP protocols** (BLE, LoRa) with **IP networks**; enables **local pre-processing (edge analytics)**.
- **How it works:** Sensor → BLE/Zigbee → **Gateway** → MQTT over TLS → Cloud.
- **Biomedical example:** A hospital **IoT medical hub** aggregates vitals from 50 ICU beds, filters noise locally, and uploads the cleaned dataset to the EMR (Electronic Medical Record) cloud.
- **Strength:** Protocol translation + local security firewall.

### 2.1.4 Back-End Data-Sharing Model
- **What:** A cloud server **redistributes** collected IoT data to *multiple authorized third-party applications* via **APIs**.
- **Why used:** Enables **interoperability** between stakeholders (hospitals, insurance, researchers, AI engines).
- **How it works:** Cloud publishes via **RESTful APIs**, **WebHooks**, or **Pub/Sub brokers** to subscribers.
- **Biomedical example:** Patient vitals on a hospital cloud are shared with — (a) the treating physician's app, (b) a **federated ML** model for sepsis prediction, and (c) the **insurance claim system**.

## 2.2 Supporting / Application-Layer Models

| Model | Flow Direction | Typical Protocol | Use-Case in Biomedical IoT |
|:------|:--------------|:-----------------|:---------------------------|
| **Publish / Subscribe** | Many → Many (via Broker) | MQTT, AMQP | Streaming vitals to many dashboards |
