<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define IoMT and list its **four/five** architectural layers. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)** is a connected infrastructure of medical devices, sensors, software, and healthcare information systems that acquire, transmit, and analyse patient data over a network without manual intervention.

The canonical architecture comprises:

1. **Perception Layer** — biosensors & wearables.
2. **Network Layer** — BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa, 5G.
3. **Edge/Fog Layer** — gateways with local intelligence.
4. **Cloud Layer** — storage, big-data analytics.
5. **Application Layer** — clinician & patient interfaces.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for layer enumeration with one example each.)*

### **Q2.** Differentiate between **Edge computing** and **Cloud computing** in an IoMT context. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Location | At gateway (hospital bed-side) | Remote data center |
| Latency | <10 ms | 50–200 ms |
| Bandwidth use | Low (local) | High (raw upload) |
| Use case | Critical alarms (arrhythmia) | Long-term trend analytics |
| Privacy | Higher (data stays local) | Lower (transit exposure) |
| Computing power | Limited (Jetson Nano) | Massive (GPU clusters) |

*(3 marks: tabular comparison covering at least 4 parameters.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — (Module 3, 14 Marks) [Recommended Choice]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **layered architecture of an IoMT system**, with one medical example for each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wearable ECG monitor transmits 3-lead ECG data sampled at **500 Hz** with **16-bit resolution**. Compute the raw data rate and discuss why local compression at the sensor is necessary before wireless transmission over BLE. **(7 marks)**

### **Question B — (Module 3, 14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the **Edge/Fog layer** in an IoMT system. With a suitable block diagram, describe how an Edge gateway performs protocol translation, local ML inference, and security enforcement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoMT sensor has a measured SNR of **20 dB** over a **1 MHz BLE channel**. Using the Shannon-Hartley theorem, compute the theoretical maximum data rate and comment on its sufficiency for streaming 8-lead ECG at 1 kHz, 12-bit resolution. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Layered Architecture**

The IoMT system architecture is organized into **five layers**:

1. **Perception Layer:** Comprises biosensors (e.g., Ag/AgCl electrodes) that transduce bioelectric events into electrical signals. *Example:* A 3-lead ECG patch worn on the chest.

2. **Network Layer:** Handles wireless transmission using protocols like **BLE 5.0** (short-range, body-area), **Wi-Fi 6** (room-scale), **LoRaWAN** (wide-area remote monitoring), or **5G URLLC** (telesurgery). *Example:* ECG patch to bedside gateway via BLE.

3. **Edge/Fog Layer:** A bedside gateway (e.g., Raspberry Pi) performs **protocol translation** (BLE → MQTT), runs **TensorFlow Lite** inference for arrhythmia detection, and applies **AES-256 encryption** before cloud upload. *Example:* Real-time ventricular fibrillation alert.

4. **Cloud Layer:** Provides **HL7 FHIR-compliant** storage, **Apache Spark** analytics for population health, and **GPU-based CNN training** on millions of ECGs. *Example:* AWS HealthLake.

5. **Application Layer:** Renders data to stakeholders — doctor's tablet showing real-time waveforms, patient's mobile app for self-monitoring, nurse-station alert boards. *Example:* Cardiologist receiving push alerts.

*(7 marks: 1 for diagram, 1 for naming layers, 5 for explanation with one example per layer.)*

#### **Part (b) — Raw Data Rate Calculation**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- Number of channels: $N = 3$
- Sampling rate: $f_s = 500 \text{ Hz}$
- Bit depth: $N_{bits} = 16$

**Step 2 — Apply raw data rate formula.**

$$D_{rate} = N \times f_s \times N_{bits}$$

$$D_{rate} = 3 \times 500 \times 16 = 24{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 24 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Discussion on compression.**
This raw rate is **manageable** for BLE (which supports up to 2 Mbps). However, in a real 12-lead clinical ECG system:

$$D_{rate}^{12-lead} = 12 \times 500 \times 16 = 96 \text{ kbps}$$

This still fits BLE, BUT when we add **headers, retransmissions, encryption overhead, and concurrent multi-patient streaming** in a ward, the effective throughput requirement balloons to **>500 kbps**, demanding **Wi-Fi**. Hence, **local on-sensor compression** (e.g., delta encoding, wavelet compression achieving 5:1 ratios) reduces 96 kbps to ~20 kbps, making BLE viable and **extending battery life** by reducing transmission duty cycle.

*(7 marks: 3 for numerical computation, 4 for engineering discussion.)*

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Role of Edge/Fog Layer**

The **Edge/Fog layer** acts as the *autonomic reflex center* of the IoMT architecture, situated between raw sensors and the distant cloud. It mitigates three critical problems:

1. **Latency:** Critical alerts (cardiac arrest, oxygen desaturation) require **<10 ms** response — impossible from cloud round-trips.
2. **Bandwidth:** Streaming raw waveforms from 100 ward patients would saturate hospital uplinks.
3. **Privacy:** Edge processing keeps identifiable raw data *inside* the hospital firewall.

**Block diagram:**

```
[Sensors] → [BLE/ZigBee RX] → [MCU Protocol Translator]
                                          ↓
                              [Edge ML Inference]
                                          ↓
                              [TLS/AES Encryption]
                                          ↓
                                [Cloud (FHIR)]
```

**Functions:**
- **Protocol Translation:** Converts vendor-specific BLE GATT attributes into standardized **HL7 FHIR Observation** resources.
- **Local ML Inference:** Runs quantized TFLite models (e.g., 1D-CNN for arrhythmia classification) on devices like NVIDIA Jetson Nano.
- **Security Enforcement:** Implements **mutual TLS (mTLS)** with X.509 certificates and stores keys in TPM modules.

*(7 marks: 2 for role, 3 for block diagram + functions, 2 for protocols and standards.)*

#### **Part (b) — Shannon Capacity for 8-Lead ECG**

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$\text{SNR} = 10^{20/10} = 10^{2} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley.**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 1 \times 10^{6} \times \log_2(101)$$

**Step 3 — Compute logarithm.**

$$\log_2(101) = \frac{\ln(101)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.615}{0.693} \approx 6.658 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Final capacity.**

$$C \approx 6.658 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5 — Required data rate for 8-lead ECG.**

$$D_{rate} = 8 \times 1000 \times 12 = 96{,}000 \text{ bps} = 96 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison and comment.**

$$\text{Capacity Utilization} = \frac{96{,}000}{6{,}658{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 1.44\%$$

The theoretical capacity (6.658 Mbps) is **~70× larger** than required — the link is **vastly over-provisioned** for this single application. This explains why BLE (even at 2 Mbps) is sufficient for ECG. The engineering insight: in IoMT, **power and battery life**, not bandwidth, are the binding constraints. Designers often sacrifice the surplus bandwidth to operate at lower PHY rates (e.g., BLE 2M → 1M PHY) to halve the current draw and double battery life.

*(7 marks: 3 for Shannon derivation, 2 for required rate, 2 for engineering commentary.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't write a generic IoT diagram.** Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if medical examples (ECG, SpO₂, glucose) are missing.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always write *kbps*, *ms*, *dB* — a missing unit loses a mark in valuation.
> 3. **Confusing "Edge" with "Cloud."** In Q1 of Part B alternatives, students often describe cloud-only operations when asked about Edge. Read carefully.
> 4. **Shannon-Hartley requires SNR in LINEAR scale.** A common error is plugging dB directly into the log. Examiners *specifically* test this conversion step.
> 5. **Not justifying "Why" compression / Why Edge.** Marks for engineering reasoning are 50% of Part B — don't just compute; *interpret*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five Layers (in order):** Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud → Application.
- **Shannon Capacity formula:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — always convert dB to linear.
- **Nyquist rule:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ for ECG: at least **250 Hz** (since ECG max ≈ 125 Hz for pediatric).
- **Latency hierarchy:** Edge <10 ms < Cloud ~200 ms — critical alerts MUST go to Edge.
- **Protocol–Use mapping:**
  - **BLE 5.0** → body-area wearables (ECG, SpO₂).
  - **Wi-Fi 6** → room-scale imaging (DICOM, ultrasound).
  - **LoRaWAN** → rural/remote chronic care.
  - **5G URLLC** → telesurgery, ambulance-to-hospital.
- **Standards triad (must memorize):** **HL7 FHIR** (data exchange), **DICOM** (imaging), **IEEE 11073** (personal health devices).
- **Security baseline:** **AES-256** at sensor + **TLS 1.3** at gateway; HIPAA + GDPR compliance mandatory.
- **Battery vs Bandwidth trade-off:** Lower PHY rate = lower power = longer life — bandwidth is rarely the bottleneck in IoMT.
- **Top 3 keywords for KTU answers:** *latency-critical*, *interoperability*, *medical-grade reliability*.
- **Equation quick-list to memorize:** Nyquist, Shannon-Hartley, raw data rate, battery energy, packet loss %, path-loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components of System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Architecture** in the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** refers to the **hierarchical, layered structural framework** that defines how heterogeneous physical devices, communication networks, data processing units, and application services interoperate to sense, transmit, process, and act upon real-world information. In biomedical engineering, this architecture specifically orchestrates **patient monitoring devices, wearable sensors, hospital information systems, and cloud-based diagnostic platforms** into a unified ecosystem.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** curriculum for *OEBMT831*, the IoT system architecture is conventionally described as a **four-layer reference model**:

1. **Perception Layer (Device / Sensor Layer)** — The physical sensing frontier.
2. **Network Layer (Transmission / Communication Layer)** — The data highway.
3. **Processing Layer (Middleware / Cloud / Edge Computing Layer)** — The brain.
4. **Application Layer (Service / Presentation Layer)** — The user-facing interface.

A fifth, often auxiliary, layer is also recognized in modern KTU modules:

5. **Business Layer (Management / Functionality Layer)** — Governs the entire system based on data analytics.

### Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Expected Terminology)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IoT Reference Layers** | **4 to 5** | Core architectural depth |
| **Frequency Bands** | **2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, Sub-1 GHz** | Common ISM band usage |
| **Latency Tolerance** | **< 5 ms (medical)** | Critical for patient monitoring |
| **Data Throughput** | **250 kbps (LoRa) to 1 Gbps (5G)** | Protocol dependent |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Nervous System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
>
> Think of an IoT system architecture exactly like the **human nervous system**:
>
> - **Sensors** (skin, eyes, ears) = **Perception Layer** — They collect stimuli from the environment.
> - **Nerves & Spinal Cord** = **Network Layer** — They carry electrical signals rapidly toward the brain.
> - **Brain (Cerebrum & Cerebellum)** = **Processing Layer** — It interprets signals, makes decisions, and triggers reactions.
> - **Muscles & Organs** = **Application Layer** — They execute the final action (writing, moving, speaking).
> - **Consciousness / Decision-Making Persona** = **Business Layer** — It governs overall behavior based on accumulated experience (data analytics).

In a **biomedical IoT setting**, imagine a **remote ECG patient monitor**:
- The **ECG electrode (sensor)** detects the heartbeat → *Perception Layer*.
- The **Bluetooth link to a gateway** transmits the data → *Network Layer*.
- A **cloud-based AI engine** detects arrhythmia → *Processing Layer*.
- The **doctor's mobile app** raises an alarm → *Application Layer*.
- The **hospital administrator** monitors all patients centrally → *Business Layer*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Structural Breakdown

### 🔹 LAYER 1 — Perception Layer (The Sensing Frontier)

This is the **lowest physical layer**, where the IoT system directly interfaces with the **physical world**.

**Core Components:**
- **Sensors**: Devices that convert a physical, chemical, or biological parameter into a measurable electrical signal.
  - *Biomedical Examples*: ECG electrodes, PPG (photoplethysmogram) sensors, SpO₂ oximeters, body temperature thermistors, accelerometers (for fall detection), glucometers.
- **Actuators**: Devices that convert electrical signals back into physical action.
  - *Biomedical Examples*: Insulin pumps, pacemakers, infusion pumps, robotic surgical arms.
- **RFID Tags & Readers**: For identification and tracking of patients, equipment, or pharmaceutical samples.
- **Embedded Microcontrollers**: Such as **Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi, NodeMCU**, which aggregate sensor data and execute local logic.

**Why does this layer matter?**
> The **fidelity, accuracy, and sampling rate** of the perception layer **directly determine** the diagnostic value of the entire IoT pipeline. **No downstream intelligence can compensate for noisy, low-resolution, or miscalibrated sensor data** (a principle often called *garbage-in, garbage-out*).

**Key Design Constraints in Biomedical Context:**
- **Biocompatibility** (ISO 10993 standards for skin-contacting devices).
- **Low power consumption** (often battery-powered, sometimes energy-harvested).
- **Miniaturization** (wearable and implantable form factors).
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** must exceed clinically accepted thresholds.

### 🔹 LAYER 2 — Network Layer (The Data Highway)

Responsible for the **reliable, secure, and timely transmission** of data between the perception layer and the processing layer.

**Core Components:**
- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **Short-range**: Bluetooth (BLE), ZigBee, NFC, Wi-Fi.
  - **Long-range**: LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M.
  - **Wired (in-hospital)**: Ethernet, RS-485, CAN bus (for medical device interconnection).
- **Gateways / Access Points**: Aggregate data from multiple sensors and forward to cloud.
- **Routers & Switches**: Manage packet routing.
- **Cellular Base Stations (4G/5G)**: For wide-area biomedical telemetry.
- **Network Management Systems**: Handle addressing (IPv4/IPv6), Quality of Service (QoS), and network slicing (especially in **5G-enabled remote surgery**).

**Communication Protocol Selection Logic:**

| Application Scenario | Recommended Protocol | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous wearable ECG | BLE 5.0 | Low power, smartphone-compatible |
| Remote village patient monitoring | LoRaWAN | Long range, very low power |
| Real-time robotic telesurgery | 5G URLLC | Ultra-low latency (< 1 ms) |
| In-hospital device bus | CAN / Ethernet | Reliability, deterministic timing |
| Patient ID wristband tracking | RFID / NFC | Proximity-based, cheap |

### 🔹 LAYER 3 — Processing Layer (The Cognitive Core)

This is where **raw data becomes meaningful information**. Often called the **"brain"** of the IoT system.

**Core Components:**
- **Edge Computing Devices**: Process data *near the source* (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano, Google Coral, smartphone processors).
  - *Why edge?* Reduces latency, preserves privacy, saves bandwidth.
- **Cloud Computing Platforms**: Centralized large-scale processing.
  - *Providers*: AWS IoT Core, Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT.
- **Fog Computing Layer**: Intermediate processing between edge and cloud.
- **Data Storage**: SQL/NoSQL databases, time-series databases (InfluxDB), data lakes.
- **Analytics & AI/ML Engines**: TensorFlow Lite, PyTorch Mobile for inference.
- **Stream Processing**: Apache Kafka, MQTT brokers (Mosquitto, EMQX).

**Biomedical Significance:**
> In **critical care IoT**, processing latency is non-negotiable. A delay in detecting **ventricular fibrillation** (cardiac arrest rhythm) of more than **5 seconds** can be fatal. Hence **edge AI inference** is preferred for such life-critical decisions, while **cloud analytics** is used for long-term epidemiological studies and population health management.

### 🔹 LAYER 4 — Application Layer (The User Interface)

The **topmost, user-facing layer** that delivers the system's value to the end-user.

**Core Components:**
- **Mobile Applications** (Android/iOS).
- **Web Dashboards** for clinicians and administrators.
- **Alerting Systems** (SMS, push notifications, alarms).
- **Visualization Tools** (Grafana, Power BI, custom dashboards).
- **API Services** for integration with Hospital Information Systems (HIS) and Electronic Medical Records (EMR).

**Biomedical Examples:**
- Real-time ECG visualization app for cardiologists.
- Diabetes management app integrating CGM (Continuous Glucose Monitor) data.
- Hospital Asset Tracking Dashboard.
- Telemedicine consultation interface.

### 🔹 LAYER 5 — Business Layer (The Governance Tier)

> [!NOTE]
> While not always counted as a "core" layer, the **Business Layer** is heavily emphasized in the **KTU 2024 Scheme** under NEP 2020 outcome-based education because it links technology to **societal impact, ethics, and sustainability**.

**Core Functions:**
- **Data-driven business model** definition.
- **Application and business rule** management.
- **Privacy, security, and regulatory compliance** (HIPAA, GDPR, India's DISHA, MDR 2017).
- **Revenue model** (subscription-based, pay-per-use, insurance-integrated).
- **Strategic decision-making** based on aggregated insights.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations and metrics are **frequently tested** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) for IoT-related subjects.

### Core Mathematical Relationships

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** | $\mathrm{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | **dB (decibels)** | Sensor signal quality |
| 2 | **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **bits/sec** | Max channel capacity |
| 3 | **Power Consumption (Tx)** | $P_t = P_{\text{PA}} + P_{\text{circuit}}$ | **Watts (W)** | Battery life estimation |
| 4 | **Battery Lifetime** | $T_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{battery}} \cdot V}{P_{\text{avg}} \cdot 3600}$ | **hours** | IoT device endurance |
| 5 | **Data Rate (Nyquist)** | $C = 2B \log_{2} M$ | **bits/sec** | Symbol-rate-limited channel |
| 6 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $\mathrm{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | **dB** | Wireless link budget (d in km, f in MHz) |
| 7 | **Sampling Theorem** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | **Hz** | Biomedical signal sampling |
| 8 | **Latency (Round-Trip)** | $L_{\text{RTT}} = 2 t_{\text{prop}} + 4 t_{\text{proc}} + \dfrac{N}{B}$ | **seconds** | Total transmission delay |
| 9 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\mathrm{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | **hours** | Reliability metric |
| 10 | **Data Volume per Patient per Day** | $V_d = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i \cdot s_i \cdot t_i)$ | **bytes/day** | Cloud storage planning |

> **Note on Notation:** In all formulas above, $P$ = power, $B$ = bandwidth, $C$ = capacity, $d$ = distance, $f$ = frequency, $N$ = number of bits, $M$ = symbol levels, $R$ = sampling rate, $s$ = sample size, $t$ = time, $\lambda$ = failure rate.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Hospitals**: Deploy this exact 5-layer architecture for ICUs, where each bed has multi-parameter sensors (Perception) → bedside monitor (Network gateway) → edge AI for arrhythmia detection (Processing) → nurse station dashboard (Application) → hospital administration (Business).
- **Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)**: Wearable sensors (Layer 1) → 4G/5G cellular link (Layer 2) → Cloud ML diagnostic engine (Layer 3) → Patient & doctor app (Layer 4) → Insurance and reimbursement logic (Layer 5).
- **Pandemic Surveillance Systems**: Used during COVID-19 for contact tracing, quarantine monitoring, and oxygen-level telemetry.
- **Clinical Trials**: IoT-architecture-enabled remote data capture improves compliance and reduces trial costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivations of High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test **derivation steps** rather than only final answers. The following derivations are written in the **exact algebraic style** expected in the ESE answer sheet.

### DERIVATION 1: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in a Biomedical IoT Sensor

> **Scenario:** A wearable ECG sensor outputs a useful cardiac signal of amplitude $A_s = 1\,\text{mV}$ with superimposed noise of amplitude $A_n = 0.05\,\text{mV}$. Compute the SNR.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.**
The signal-to-noise ratio compares the **power** of the signal to the **power** of the noise. Power is proportional to the **square of the amplitude** (for a given load impedance $R$).

$$P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{A_s^{\,2}}{R}$$

$$P_{\text{noise}} = \frac{A_n^{\,2}}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio.**
Because both terms share the same load impedance $R$, it cancels in the ratio.

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{A_s^{\,2}}{A_n^{\,2}} = \left(\frac{A_s}{A_n}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\frac{A_s}{A_n} = \frac{1\,\text{mV}}{0.05\,\text{mV}} = 20$$

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = (20)^{2} = 400$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels.**
The KTU board expects the **dB form** of SNR.

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(400)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\log_{10}(400) = \log_{10}(4 \times 10^{2}) = \log_{10}(4) + 2 \approx 0.6021 + 2 = 2.6021$$

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 2.6021 \approx 26.02\,\text{dB}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** $\mathrm{SNR} \approx \mathbf{26.02\,\text{dB}}$, which is **excellent** for biomedical ECG acquisition (clinical threshold is typically $\geq 20\,\text{dB}$).

---

### DERIVATION 2: Battery Lifetime of a Wearable IoT Device

> **Scenario:** A wearable pulse oximeter is powered by a 3.7 V lithium-polymer battery of capacity 400 mAh. The device draws an average current of 8 mA during continuous operation. Estimate the battery lifetime.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula.**

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$$

The result is in **hours** when capacity is in **mAh** and current is in **mA**.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{400\,\text{mAh}}{8\,\text{mA}} = 50\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to days for clinical scheduling.**

$$50\,\text{hours} = \frac{50}{24}\,\text{days} \approx 2.08\,\text{days}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** The battery lasts approximately **50 hours (≈ 2 days)** of continuous operation. For practical deployment, the IoT firmware should implement **duty cycling** (e.g., active 5 s, sleep 55 s) to extend this to several weeks.

**Duty-Cycled Extension (advanced):** If the active duty cycle is $D = 0.1$ (10 %), then

$$T_{\text{life}}^{\text{DC}} = \frac{T_{\text{life}}}{D} = \frac{50\,\text{h}}{0.1} = 500\,\text{hours} \approx 20.8\,\text{days}$$

---

### DERIVATION 3: Channel Capacity for a Wireless Biomedical Link (Shannon-Hartley)

> **Scenario:** A 5G small cell provides telemetry to an ambulance. The allocated bandwidth is $B = 20\,\text{MHz}$ and the SNR is $30\,\text{dB}$. Compute the maximum theoretical data rate.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$C = 20 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}(1 + 1000) = 20 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}(1001)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\log_{2}(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967$$

**Step 5 — Multiply to obtain the bit rate.**

$$C = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 9.967 \approx 199.34 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bits/s}$$

$$C \approx 199.34\,\text{Mbps}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** The link can support up to **≈ 199.3 Mbps**, which is more than sufficient for **real-time HD ultrasound streaming** from the ambulance to the hospital.

---

### DERIVATION 4: Sampling Rate for ECG and EMG (Nyquist Theorem)

> **Scenario:** Determine the minimum sampling frequency for an ECG signal (bandwidth ≈ 150 Hz) and an EMG signal (bandwidth ≈ 500 Hz).

**Step 1 — State the Nyquist criterion.**

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to ECG.**

$$f_s^{\text{ECG}} \geq 2 \times 150 = 300\,\text{Hz}$$

In clinical practice, the KTU-expected standard is **360 Hz or 500 Hz** (chosen as a safety margin).

**Step 3 — Apply to EMG.**

$$f_s^{\text{EMG}} \geq 2 \times 500 = 1000\,\text{Hz}$$

Clinical standard: **1 kHz to 2 kHz**, with high-resolution EMG going up to **10 kHz**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU Board-Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> The following decision matrix is the **most frequently asked comparative analysis** in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for IoT.

| Selection Criterion | **Bluetooth (BLE)** | **ZigBee** | **Wi-Fi** | **LoRaWAN** | **NB-IoT** | **5G** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | ~10 m | ~100 m | ~100 m | ~15 km | ~10 km | ~10 km |
| **Data Rate** | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 1 Gbps | 50 kbps | 200 kbps | 10 Gbps |
| **Power** | Very Low | Very Low | High | Very Low | Low | Medium |
| **Latency** | 7.5 ms | 30 ms | 5 ms | 1–2 s | 1.6–10 s | < 1 ms |
| **Best Biomedical Use** | Wearables | Hospital mesh | Telemedicine hubs | Rural RPM | Asset tracking | Telesurgery |

**KTU Examiner's Heuristic (memorize this):**
> If the question says **"continuous, low-power, short-range patient monitoring"** → **BLE**.
> If the question says **"long-range village deployment"** → **LoRaWAN**.
> If the question says **"real-time robotic surgery"** → **5G URLLC**.
> If the question says **"in-hospital dense device mesh"** → **ZigBee**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Data Flow Sequence in a Cardiac Monitoring Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Patient
    participant Sensor as ECG Sensor L1
    participant Gateway as BLE Gateway L2
    participant Edge as Edge AI L3
    participant Cloud as Cloud Server L3
    participant App as Doctor App L4
    participant Admin as Hospital Admin L5

    Patient->>Sensor: Heart generates electrical signal
    Sensor->>Sensor: Sample at 500 Hz
    Sensor->>Gateway: Transmit via BLE 5.0
    Gateway->>Edge: Forward raw waveform

        Edge->>Cloud: Upload compressed summary
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the four-layer IoT system architecture. List the primary function of each layer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four-layer IoT system architecture, as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, consists of:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensor Layer):** The physical layer that uses sensors and actuators to collect data from the environment (e.g., ECG, SpO₂, temperature).
2. **Network Layer (Communication Layer):** Transmits the collected data via wired or wireless protocols (BLE, Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRaWAN) to the processing infrastructure.
3. **Processing Layer (Middleware Layer):** Performs data storage, analytics, and decision-making using cloud, fog, or edge computing resources.
4. **Application Layer (Service Layer):** Delivers the processed information to end-users through apps, dashboards, or alerting services.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Edge Computing and Cloud Computing in the context of IoT. State one biomedical use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | **Edge Computing** | **Cloud Computing** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Location of processing** | At or near the data source | In a remote data centre |
| **Latency** | Very low (< 10 ms) | Higher (50–500 ms) |
| **Bandwidth use** | Minimal | High (raw data upload) |
| **Privacy** | Higher (data stays local) | Lower (data leaves premises) |
| **Biomedical Use Case** | Real-time arrhythmia detection in a wearable ECG | Long-term population health analytics for hospital administrators |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-layer IoT architecture** suitable for a **Smart Hospital ICU patient monitoring system**. Identify the components at each layer and justify your choice of communication protocol for the network layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same smart hospital system, the ICU monitors 50 patients, each generating ECG data sampled at 500 Hz with 16-bit resolution, transmitted continuously to the cloud. Calculate the **total daily data volume in gigabytes (GB)** generated by the ICU.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Smart Hospital ICU Architecture (7 Marks)**

**Block Diagram (text representation, to be drawn in exam):**

```
[ECG/EEG/SpO₂ Sensors]
        ↓
[Bedside Monitor with BLE 5.0]
        ↓
[ZigBee Hospital Backbone Mesh]
        ↓
[Edge AI Server (NVIDIA Jetson) — Arrhythmia Detection]
        ↓
[Cloud (AWS/Azure) — Long-term Storage, Analytics]
        ↓
[Doctor Mobile App, Nurse Dashboard, EMR Integration]
        ↓
[Hospital Admin, Insurance, Compliance]
```

**Layer-wise Component Identification (1.5 marks per layer × 5 layers ≈ 7.5 marks, capped to 7):**

| Layer | Components | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Perception** | Multi-parameter patient monitor: 5-lead ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, temperature, respiration sensor | Captures all vital signs required for ICU care |
| **Network** | **BLE 5.0** at bedside + **ZigBee mesh** to nurse station + **Ethernet backhaul** to cloud | BLE minimizes cabling, ZigBee enables low-power mesh, Ethernet provides reliability for backhaul |
| **Processing** | **Edge AI** (Jetson) for instant arrhythmia detection + **Cloud** (AWS IoT) for archival | Edge handles life-critical sub-5-second alarms; cloud handles analytics and long-term trends |
| **Application** | Tablet-based ICU dashboard, doctor's iPad app, alert paging system | Provides real-time visual feedback to clinicians |
| **Business** | HIS/EMR integration, billing engine, regulatory compliance module | Aligns with hospital workflows and legal frameworks |

**Protocol Justification for Network Layer (additional 1 mark):**
> BLE 5.0 is chosen because it offers **2 Mbps throughput** (sufficient for 16-bit ECG), **ultra-low power** for bedside operation, and **compatibility with existing smartphones** used by clinicians. ZigBee forms the **mesh backbone** because of its **self-healing topology** and ability to interconnect **hundreds of nodes** without infrastructure overload.

---

#### **Part (b) — Daily Data Volume Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Calculate per-second data rate for one patient.**

$$R_{\text{1s}} = f_s \times n_{\text{bits}} = 500\,\text{Hz} \times 16\,\text{bits} = 8000\,\text{bits/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes per second.**

$$R_{\text{1s, bytes}} = \frac{8000}{8} = 1000\,\text{bytes/sec}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate per-day data volume for ONE patient.**

$$V_{\text{1 patient}} = 1000\,\text{bytes/sec} \times 86400\,\text{sec/day} = 86{,}400{,}000\,\text{bytes/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MB.**

$$V_{\text{1 patient}} = \frac{86{,}400{,}000}{10^{6}} = 86.4\,\text{MB/day}$$

**Step 5 — Scale to 50 patients.**

$$V_{\text{50 patients}} = 50 \times 86.4\,\text{MB} = 4320\,\text{MB/day}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GB.**

$$V_{\text{ICU, daily}} = \frac{4320}{1024} \approx 4.219\,\text{GB/day}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** The ICU generates approximately **4.22 GB of ECG data per day** for 50 patients. **Storage implication:** Over one year, this is **≈ 1.54 TB**, justifying the need for **lossless compression** (e.g., wavelet-based ECG compression achieving 10:1 ratios) and **tiered storage** (hot SSD for recent 30 days, cold HDD/object storage for older records).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, and 5G** as communication protocols for biomedical IoT, with a comparative table and one specific medical application for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An IoT-enabled smart insulin pump operates on a 3.7 V, 600 mAh battery. The active mode consumes 25 mA and the sleep mode consumes 0.5 mA. The firmware uses a duty cycle of 5 % active and 95 % sleep. Estimate the **battery lifetime in days**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Protocol Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Latency | Medical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BLE 5.0** | 10 m | 2 Mbps | Very Low | 7.5 ms | Wearable ECG patch |
| **ZigBee** | 100 m | 250 kbps | Very Low | 30 ms | Hospital mesh for low-rate vitals |
| **Wi-Fi 6** | 100 m | 1 Gbps | High | 5 ms | Telemedicine video consultation |
| **LoRaWAN** | 15 km | 50 kbps | Very Low | 1–2 s | Rural village RPM |
| **5G URLLC** | 10 km | 10 Gbps | Medium | < 1 ms | Remote robotic telesurgery |

**Application Justification (3 marks — 0.5 per protocol):**
- **BLE** → Continuous heart rate monitoring via a wristband that pairs with the patient's phone.
- **ZigBee** → Mesh network in a hospital ward transmitting periodic body temperature from every bed.
- **Wi-Fi** → Doctor–patient video consultation in OPD requiring HD video streaming.
- **LoRaWAN** → Outdoor patient tracking and chronic-disease monitoring in remote areas lacking cellular coverage.
- **5G URLLC** → Surgeon in one city controls a robotic arm in another city with haptic feedback.

---

#### **Part (b) — Battery Lifetime with Duty Cycling (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$$

where $I_{\text{avg}}$ is the time-weighted average current.

**Step 2 — Compute the average current with duty cycle.**

$$I_{\text{avg}} = (I_{\text{active}} \cdot D) + (I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - D))$$

$$I_{\text{avg}} = (25\,\text{mA} \times 0.05) + (0.5\,\text{mA} \times 0.95)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the products.**

$$I_{\text{avg}} = 1.25\,\text{mA} + 0.475\,\text{mA} = 1.725\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute battery lifetime in hours.**

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{600\,\text{mAh}}{1.725\,\text{mA}} \approx 347.83\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to days.**

$$T_{\text{life, days}} = \frac{347.83}{24} \approx 14.49\,\text{days}$$

> ✅ **Final Answer:** The smart insulin pump will last approximately **14.5 days** on a single charge — adequate for a two-week patient cycle, with a small safety margin.

**Engineering Insight (additional 1 mark):**
> If the duty cycle were reduced to 2 % (typical for "one insulin bolus per 30 minutes"), the lifetime would extend to ≈ **34.5 days**, demonstrating the **exponential benefit** of aggressive power management in biomedical implants.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing Layers:** Students often **swap Perception and Application layers** while writing. Always remember: *Perception = physical sensors; Application = user interface.*
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** Many lose 0.5–1 mark by writing SNR as a pure number instead of **decibels (dB)**, or data volume as bytes instead of **MB/GB**.
> 3. **No Justification:** Simply naming a protocol without explaining *why* it is chosen is penalized. Always provide **at least one reason** (range, power, latency, cost).
> 4. **Duty Cycle Misconception:** Students frequently compute $I_{\text{avg}} = I_{\text{active}} \times D$ only, ignoring sleep current. **Sleep current is non-zero and matters** in long-life IoT devices.
> 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** In 7-mark questions, **a labelled diagram is mandatory**. A text-only answer caps you at ~5 marks.
> 6. **In (b) part numerical questions:** Always **show the substitution step explicitly**. Writing only the final answer without intermediate steps loses 50 % of the allocated marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 10-Golden-Point Summary (memorize verbatim for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- ⭐ **Architecture has 4 mandatory layers + 1 business layer**: Perception, Network, Processing, Application, Business.
- ⭐ **Perception Layer** = sensors + actuators + RFID; in biomedicine → ECG, EEG, EMG, SpO₂, temperature, accelerometers.
- ⭐ **Network Layer** protocol selection follows the **"range vs. power vs. latency"** triangle: BLE for wearables, LoRa for villages, 5G for surgery.
- ⭐ **Processing Layer** can be **edge (low latency), fog (intermediate), or cloud (heavy analytics)** — never use cloud for life-critical sub-second decisions.
- ⭐ **Application Layer** delivers the value — clinician dashboards, patient apps, EMR integration, alerting systems.
- ⭐ **Business Layer** ensures compliance (HIPAA, GDPR, India's DISHA) and links technology to revenue/society.
- ⭐ **SNR in dB**: $\mathrm{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$. Biomedical ECG standard ≥ **20 dB**.
- ⭐ **Nyquist Sampling**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$. ECG → 360–500 Hz; EMG → 1–2 kHz; EEG → 256–512 Hz.
- ⭐ **Battery Life with Duty Cycle**: $I_{\text{avg}} = I_a D + I_s(1-D)$; then $T = C_b / I_{\text{avg}}$.
- ⭐ **Data Volume**: $V = R_s \times b \times t \times N_{\text{patients}}$; always convert to MB/GB for the final answer.

> **Last-Memorize Mnemonic:** **"PNPAB"** → **P**erception → **N**etwork → **P**rocessing → **A**pplication → **B**usiness. The data flow is *bottom-up* (sensors to apps) but the *control flow* is *top-down* (admin configures sensors).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use Cases of IoT in Healthcare: Reducing Waiting Time in Emergency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Use Cases of IoT in Healthcare: Reducing Waiting Time in Emergency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEBMT831 – IoT & Biomedical Applications**, the use case of **"Reducing Waiting Time in Emergency"** is formally defined as:

> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> An IoT-enabled emergency healthcare ecosystem is a cyber-physical system of interconnected biomedical sensors, edge gateways, cloud analytics, and hospital information systems that collectively perform *real-time patient triage, ambulance telemetry, bed/resource discovery, and dynamic priority scheduling* — with the primary engineering objective of minimizing the **patient-to-treatment latency** (often quantified as the **Door-to-Needle Time** or **Door-to-Balloon Time** in clinical literature).

The core engineering problem addressed is the reduction of the **Emergency Department (ED) Length of Stay (ED-LOS)** through continuous, asynchronous, and remote physiological data acquisition — replacing the legacy reactive workflow (patient arrives → manual registration → vitals taken → doctor available) with a **proactive, pre-emptive data pipeline**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Healthcare Use Cases):**
> Students must be able to (i) identify IoT subsystems in an emergency workflow, (ii) map sensing layers to clinical decision points, and (iii) justify the reduction in waiting time using quantitative metrics. Memorize the acronyms **ED-LOS**, **EMS (Emergency Medical Services)**, **EMS-to-Balloon**, and **mHealth (mobile Health)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a busy airport check-in counter. In the **old model**, you stand in a long physical queue. The airline staff only learns who you are and where you are flying when you reach the counter. In the **IoT-enabled model** (think of modern mobile check-in with live bag-drop allocation), the moment you book your ticket, your *identity, weight, destination, frequent-flier tier, and connecting flight* are already broadcast to every relevant counter, baggage belt, and security gate. By the time you physically arrive, the system has *pre-allocated a slot, a gate, and a handler for you*.

**Healthcare Emergency IoT works exactly like this.** Instead of a patient arriving at the hospital and *then* being registered and assessed, their **vitals are captured inside the ambulance**, transmitted in real time, and processed in the cloud so that by the time the ambulance physically reaches the hospital gate:

- The **right doctor** has already been alerted.
- The **right equipment** (defibrillator, CT scanner, ventilator) is pre-staged.
- A **bed/ICU slot** is already reserved.
- The **patient's EMR (Electronic Medical Record)** is auto-pulled.

The "waiting time" is therefore collapsed — the patient effectively **skips the triage queue** because triage has already been performed *in transit*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realization:** The waiting time is not reduced by working *faster* inside the hospital. It is reduced by *moving the start line of clinical decision-making out of the hospital* — into the ambulance, the patient's home (via wearables), and the cloud.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants & KPIs)

The following are the **standardized clinical and engineering KPIs** universally referenced in KTU board questions on this topic:

| Acronym | Full Form | Typical Target Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ED-LOS** | Emergency Department Length of Stay | **< 4 hours** (international ED benchmark) | Total time from arrival to discharge/admission |
| **D2B** | Door-to-Balloon Time | **< 90 minutes** | For STEMI (heart attack) — gold-standard quality metric |
| **D2N** | Door-to-Needle Time | **< 60 minutes** | For thrombolysis in stroke |
| **EMS-TT** | EMS Turnaround Time | **< 15 minutes** (urban) | Time ambulance is blocked at hospital |
| **NEWS2** | National Early Warning Score 2 | Threshold **≥ 7** | Triage priority trigger |
| **mEWS** | modified Early Warning Score | Threshold **≥ 4** | Pre-hospital triage score |

These metrics serve as the **measurement baseline** against which any IoT intervention is justified in a KTU 14-mark answer.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The IoT-driven reduction of emergency waiting time is best understood as a **five-layer pipeline**. Each layer is responsible for collapsing one segment of the legacy waiting period.

### Layer 1 — The Sensing Layer (Perception)
- **Wearable biomedical sensors** worn by the patient (smartwatch with PPG/ECG, smart patch, glucometer, SpO₂ ring) continuously capture vitals: **HR, SpO₂, BP, ECG, body temperature, motion/fall detection**.
- **In-ambulance sensors**: portable ECG (12-lead), pulse oximeter, capnograph, blood-gas analyser.
- **Environmental sensors** in the home: motion sensors detecting falls in the elderly; smart-medicine-box sensors detecting missed cardiac medication.

> **Why it matters:** Detection is moved from "after hospital arrival" to "in the field." The clock effectively starts the moment the patient becomes critical, not when they reach the hospital.

### Layer 2 — The Communication Layer (Transmission)
- Data is transmitted using a **heterogeneous network stack**:
  - **PAN / BAN** (Personal/Body Area Network): BLE 5.0, Zigbee, NFC.
  - **LAN**: Wi-Fi 6 inside the hospital.
  - **WAN**: 4G/5G/LTE-M/NB-IoT for the in-transit ambulance link.
- Critical vitals use **MQTT-SN over 5G** with QoS Level 2 (exactly-once delivery) — to guarantee the cardiologist's tablet receives the ECG strip without packet loss.

### Layer 3 — The Edge / Fog Layer (Local Pre-Processing)
- The **ambulance onboard edge gateway** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi 4 + FPGA accelerator) runs a **lightweight ML model** (e.g., a 1D-CNN for arrhythmia classification).
- It computes the **mEWS score** in real time and decides: *transmit as "yellow alert" or "red alert"*. This **filters noise and prioritises bandwidth** — only abnormal events hit the cloud.

### Layer 4 — The Cloud / Analytics Layer
- A **cloud-based Hospital Information System (HIS)** receives the alert and executes a **rule engine**:
  1. Auto-pulls the patient's EMR from the database.
  2. Geolocates the ambulance via GPS and estimates **ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival)**.
  3. Cross-references available **ICU beds, cath-lab slots, and on-call specialists** using a **Resource Availability Lookup (RAL)** microservice.
  4. Pushes a *pre-admission packet* to the receiving ED team.

### Layer 5 — The Actuation / Notification Layer
- The ED doctor's tablet buzzes with: *"STEMI suspected, ETA 7 min, Cath-Lab 2 reserved, Dr. X notified, Patient blood group O+ from EMR."*
- A digital **token / queue token** is reserved for the patient. Upon physical arrival, the patient bypasses manual registration.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Quantifying the Time Saved

Let $T_{legacy}$ be the legacy waiting time and $T_{IoT}$ the IoT-enabled waiting time. We can decompose both:

$$T_{legacy} = t_{reg} + t_{triage} + t_{diag} + t_{consult} + t_{treat}$$

$$T_{IoT} = t_{pre\text{-}triage} + t_{treat}$$

where each segment has the following meaning:

| Symbol | Segment | Typical Legacy Duration | IoT-Enabled Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_{reg}$ | Manual registration | **8–15 min** | $\approx 0$ (auto from EMR) |
| $t_{triage}$ | Vitals + nurse assessment | **5–10 min** | **0** (done in ambulance) |
| $t_{diag}$ | Doctor's diagnosis from vitals | **10–20 min** | **2–5 min** (pre-staged) |
| $t_{consult}$ | Specialist consultation | **15–30 min** | **0–5 min** (alerted in advance) |
| $t_{treat}$ | Actual treatment | Constant | Constant (cannot be compressed) |

The **time compression** achieved is therefore:

$$\Delta T = T_{legacy} - T_{IoT} \approx 30\text{ to }65 \text{ minutes}$$

For a STEMI patient, where every minute of delay causes **~1% additional myocardial damage**, $\Delta T = 45$ min saves roughly **45% of the heart muscle** — a clinically massive outcome from an engineering intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight for KTU Answers:**
> The waiting time reduction is **not a single-tech achievement** — it is the *compounding* effect of (a) data acquisition moved earlier, (b) latency compressed by 5G, (c) decisions automated by edge ML, and (d) resources pre-allocated by cloud orchestration. Examiners reward answers that explicitly name **all four mechanisms**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ED Length of Stay (ED-LOS) | $ED\text{-}LOS = t_{discharge} - t_{arrival}$ | **minutes** | Baseline KPI |
| 2 | Door-to-Balloon Time (D2B) | $D2B = t_{balloon} - t_{door}$ | **minutes** | STEMI quality metric |
| 3 | Door-to-Needle Time (D2N) | $D2N = t_{needle} - t_{door}$ | **minutes** | Stroke quality metric |
| 4 | NEWS2 Score | $S_{NEWS2} = \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \cdot s_i$ | **dimensionless** | Triage priority (0–20) |
| 5 | Time Saved | $\Delta T = T_{legacy} - T_{IoT}$ | **minutes** | Justification metric |
| 6 | % Reduction in Wait | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{legacy}} \times 100$ | **%** | KTU 14-mark answer headline |
| 7 | ML Edge Inference Latency | $\tau_{edge} = \dfrac{F}{f_{clock}} \cdot N_{cores}^{-1}$ | **ms** | Edge AI budget |
| 8 | 5G End-to-End Latency (URLLC) | $L_{5G} \le 1$ | **ms** | Real-time ECG uplink |
| 9 | Ambulance ETA | $ETA = \dfrac{d_{remaining}}{\bar{v}_{avg}}$ | **minutes** | Resource pre-staging |
| 10 | Bandwidth (ECG stream) | $BW = f_{s} \times N_{bits} \times N_{ch}$ | **kbps** | Network design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder:** All clinical times are in **minutes**, never hours, when answering KTU numerical questions. All network latencies are in **milliseconds (ms)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

This use case is a textbook example of a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** meeting **Telenursing** meets **Health Informatics**. The same architectural pattern is reused in:

- **Smart City traffic pre-emption** (green-corridor for ambulances — GPS + traffic IoT).
- **Industrial predictive maintenance** (sensor detects anomaly → cloud books a maintenance slot).
- **Disaster response robotics** (UAVs broadcast survivor vitals to the receiving trauma center).
- **Automotive V2X** (a car crash e-Call automatically dispatches an ambulance with the crash telemetry).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivating the ED-LOS Reduction Model (Detailed)

The KTU 14-mark answer often requires you to *derive* a quantitative expression for the wait-time reduction. The following is a complete, step-by-step derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the legacy timeline
Let the patient's journey in a **non-IoT** hospital begin at $t = 0$ (ED door entry). Each stage has an associated stochastic latency.

$$T_{legacy} = t_{reg} + t_{triage} + t_{diag} + t_{consult} + t_{treat}$$

### Step 2 — Define the IoT-enabled timeline
In an IoT-enabled system, the *first four* stages are completed **before** $t = 0$ (i.e., during ambulance transit). Only the immutable $t_{treat}$ remains at $t \ge 0$.

$$T_{IoT} = t_{treat} + \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small residual overhead ($\epsilon \approx 1$ to $3$ min) for final physical handover and ID verification.

### Step 3 — Subtract to obtain the time saving

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{legacy} - T_{IoT} \\
         &= (t_{reg} + t_{triage} + t_{diag} + t_{consult} + t_{treat}) - (t_{treat} + \epsilon) \\
         &= t_{reg} + t_{triage} + t_{diag} + t_{consult} - \epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Insert realistic clinical values

For a typical urban Indian ED:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{reg}   &= 10 \text{ min} \\
t_{triage} &= 7 \text{ min} \\
t_{diag}  &= 15 \text{ min} \\
t_{consult} &= 20 \text{ min} \\
\epsilon   &= 2 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 10 + 7 + 15 + 20 - 2 \\
         &= 50 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the percentage improvement

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\Delta T}{T_{legacy}} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{50}{52 + t_{treat}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

For $t_{treat} = 30$ min:

$$\eta = \frac{50}{82} \times 100 \approx 60.97\%$$

> **Interpretation:** The IoT intervention compresses the *non-treatment* portion of the ED journey by **~61%**, while leaving the actual treatment duration untouched. This is the **cleanest, most defensible KTU answer** for "by how much is the waiting time reduced?"

### Step 6 — Compute the bandwidth budget for the ECG uplink
For a 12-lead ECG sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz with $N_{bits} = 12$ bits/sample:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{ECG} &= f_s \times N_{bits} \times N_{ch} \\
         &= 500 \times 12 \times 12 \\
         &= 72{,}000 \text{ bits/sec} \\
         &= 72 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding pulse-oximeter (1 kbps), BP cuff (0.1 kbps) and capnograph (5 kbps):

$$BW_{total} \approx 78 \text{ kbps per ambulance}$$

This fits comfortably inside a **5G URLLC slice** (guaranteed 1 ms latency, 10 Mbps uplink).

### Step 3.2 — Analytical Matrix: IoT Function vs Waiting-Time Segment Reduced

| Waiting-Time Segment (Legacy) | IoT Subsystem Responsible | % of Total Wait Reduced | KTUI Marker |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manual registration | Auto-EMR fetch from Aadhaar-linked EHR | **~12%** | High-yield |
| Vitals measurement triage | Ambulance + wearable pre-acquisition | **~9%** | High-yield |
| Diagnostic delay | Edge ML inference (arrhythmia/stroke flag) | **~18%** | High-yield |
| Specialist consultation latency | Push alert to on-call specialist's tablet | **~24%** | High-yield |
| Resource (bed/equipment) search | Cloud-side RAL (Resource Availability Lookup) | **~15%** | Medium-yield |
| Patient identification errors | RFID/NFC wristband issued in ambulance | **~3%** | Low-yield |
| **Net Improvement (η)** | Compounded effect of all subsystems | **~60%** | **Boards favourite** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** A KTU 14-mark answer that uses a **tabular analytical matrix** like the above is virtually guaranteed to cross the 10-mark threshold because it demonstrates *system-level thinking* — not just rote definitions.
