# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
Define the term **ED-LOS** in the context of IoT-enabled emergency healthcare. List **two** IoT interventions that directly reduce ED-LOS.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** ED-LOS, or *Emergency Department Length of Stay*, is the total time elapsed between a patient's arrival at the ED door and their final disposition (discharge, admission, or transfer). The international benchmark is **less than 4 hours**.
- **Two IoT interventions (2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks):**
  1. **In-ambulance vital sign acquisition** combined with **edge-AI triage** (mEWS scoring) that pre-empts the in-hospital triage stage.
  2. **Cloud-based EMR auto-fetch and bed/equipment pre-allocation**, which eliminates the legacy manual registration and resource-search delays.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
What is **Door-to-Balloon (D2B) time**? Why is reducing it considered the *gold standard* of emergency cardiology metrics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **D2B definition (1.5 marks):** Door-to-Balloon time is the interval between a STEMI (ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction) patient's arrival at the hospital door and the inflation of the angioplasty balloon inside the blocked coronary artery. The target is **under 90 minutes**.
- **Why it is the gold standard (1.5 marks):** In STEMI, cardiac muscle dies at a rate of ~1% per minute of delay. D2B therefore directly correlates with **infarct size, 30-day mortality, and long-term ejection fraction**. It is the most rigorously audited emergency metric, and any IoT system that demonstrably reduces D2B is treated as a Class-I clinical evidence intervention.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the five-layer IoT architecture used to reduce waiting time in an emergency department. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five-layer architecture is:

1. **Perception Layer (1.5 marks):** Biomedical sensors — wearable ECG patch, in-ambulance 12-lead ECG, pulse oximeter, BP cuff, accelerometer-based fall detector, and a camera for trauma assessment. These convert physical vitals into electrical/digital signals.

2. **Network / Communication Layer (1.5 marks):** Heterogeneous communication stack — BLE 5.0 inside the BAN, 5G URLLC for the ambulance-to-cloud uplink (latency ≤ **1 ms**), and Wi-Fi 6 inside the hospital. MQTT-SN is used as the lightweight pub-sub protocol.

3. **Edge / Fog Layer (1.5 marks):** Onboard edge gateway (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano) running a 1D-CNN classifier that detects arrhythmias in real time. The layer computes the mEWS score locally, filters noise, and forwards only abnormal events to the cloud, thereby saving bandwidth.

4. **Cloud / Analytics Layer (1.5 marks):** Hospital Information System (HIS) hosted on a HIPAA-compliant cloud. Performs EMR lookup via FHIR API, executes a Rule Engine for triage priority, and runs a Resource Availability Lookup (RAL) microservice to reserve ICU beds and cath-lab slots.

5. **Application / Actuation Layer (1 mark):** ED dashboards, doctor tablet push notifications, smart pre-admission tokens, and physical actuators (smart bed, smart IV pump) that finalise the pre-staging.

*Neat block diagram (additional requirement — awarded in the 7-mark head):* The diagram must show **all five layers** in a clear stacked format, with arrows from sensors → network → edge → cloud → application.

---

**(b) A legacy ED process takes 12 min for registration, 7 min for triage, 15 min for diagnostic workup, and 20 min for specialist consultation before treatment begins. The IoT-enabled system performs all four activities in transit with a 2-min residual handover. Compute (i) the legacy total wait, (ii) the IoT-enabled wait, and (iii) the percentage reduction in waiting time. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Legacy wait (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{legacy} &= t_{reg} + t_{triage} + t_{diag} + t_{consult} + t_{treat} \\
           &= 12 + 7 + 15 + 20 + t_{treat}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $t_{treat} = 30$ min (intervention time):

$$T_{legacy} = 12 + 7 + 15 + 20 + 30 = 84 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — IoT-enabled wait (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{IoT} &= t_{treat} + \epsilon \\
        &= 30 + 2 = 32 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{T_{legacy} - T_{IoT}}{T_{legacy}} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{84 - 32}{84} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{52}{84} \times 100 \\
     &\approx 61.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The IoT-enabled system reduces the total pre-treatment waiting time from **84 min to 32 min**, a **~61.9% improvement**, primarily by relocating the *triage, registration, diagnostic, and consultation* phases into the in-transit period.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the role of edge computing and the mEWS score in reducing emergency waiting time. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Edge Computing Role (3.5 marks):**
Edge computing refers to performing data processing **at or near the data source** (the ambulance) rather than sending raw data to a distant cloud. In an emergency IoT pipeline, the edge gateway:
1. Receives raw ECG, SpO₂, and BP streams from biomedical sensors via BLE.
2. Runs a **lightweight 1D-CNN inference** (e.g., 12 MB model) to detect arrhythmias within **~20–50 ms**.
3. Computes the **mEWS (modified Early Warning Score)** from the vitals.
4. Decides whether to forward the data as a *yellow* (priority) or *red* (immediate) alert, thereby **reducing cloud bandwidth by ~80%** and **shrinking clinical decision latency from seconds (cloud round-trip) to milliseconds (local)**.

**mEWS Score Role (3.5 marks):**
The **modified Early Warning Score (mEWS)** is a numerical aggregate of six physiological parameters — *respiratory rate, SpO₂, heart rate, systolic BP, temperature, and level of consciousness* — each scored 0–3. The total ranges from 0 to 14. A score of **≥ 4** triggers an automatic high-priority alert to the cloud, which in turn activates the IoT pre-admission pipeline (bed reservation, specialist notification). Thus the mEWS acts as the **single quantitative threshold** that bridges *sensing* and *actuation*, removing the subjectivity and delay of manual triage.

---

**(b) Illustrate with a sequence diagram how an IoT-enabled smart ambulance communicates with the hospital to compress the door-to-balloon time for a STEMI patient. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The sequence diagram should contain the following chronological events (each event ≈ 1 mark):

| Time | Event | Actor → Actor |
|---|---|---|
| T+0 min | Patient collapses; vitals captured | Patient → Ambulance sensors |
| T+0.5 min | Vitals stream begins (ECG 500 Hz, SpO₂ 1 Hz) | Ambulance → Edge gateway |
| T+1 min | Edge AI flags **STEMI** (ST-elevation in lead II) | Edge → Edge (internal) |
| T+1.5 min | mEWS score computed (= 8, critical) | Edge → Edge (internal) |
| T+2 min | **Red alert** pushed to cloud over 5G URLLC | Edge → Cloud HIS |
| T+2.5 min | EMR auto-fetched via FHIR API | Cloud → Cloud |
| T+3 min | **Cath-Lab 2** reserved; on-call cardiologist paged | Cloud → Hospital resources |
| T+3.5 min | Cardiologist acknowledges on tablet; ETA estimated 6 min | Cardiologist → Cloud |
| T+9.5 min | Ambulance arrives; patient **bypasses ED queue**; goes directly to Cath-Lab 2 | Ambulance → Hospital |
| T+10.5 min | Balloon inflated | Hospital |

**Door-to-Balloon achieved = 1 min (door arrival to balloon inflation), well within the 90-min gold standard.**

The diagram (drawn on paper) must show the participants on the *y-axis* and time on the *x-axis*, with arrows representing messages. Award the 7th mark for the **concluding sentence** explicitly stating the D2B outcome.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "waiting time" with "treatment time."** Treatment time ($t_{treat}$) is **incompressible** — do not claim IoT reduces it. IoT reduces the *non-treatment* waiting segments.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always state minutes for clinical times and milliseconds for network latency. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for missing units in 14-mark numerical answers.
> 3. **Naming only one or two IoT layers.** A 7-mark architecture answer must mention **all five layers** (Perception, Network, Edge, Cloud, Application). Mentioning only sensors + cloud is incomplete.
> 4. **Using the term "AI" without specifying the model.** Saying "AI detects arrhythmia" is vague. Say "**a 1D-CNN with 12 MB footprint** running on **edge**." This signals depth.
> 5. **Forgetting the human-in-the-loop caveat.** IoT *prepares* the decision; the *clinical authority* still rests with the doctor. Examiners reward answers that acknowledge this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ED-LOS** is the master KPI; the IoT objective is to keep it **below 4 hours**.
- **Door-to-Balloon (D2B) < 90 min** and **Door-to-Needle (D2N) < 60 min** are the gold-standard cardiology and neurology metrics — memorise both.
- The waiting time is compressed by **moving four activities (registration, triage, diagnosis, consultation) into the in-transit period**, not by speeding up the hospital.
- The **five-layer IoT architecture** (Perception → Network → Edge → Cloud → Application) is the standard KTU-required block diagram. Draw it neatly, label every layer.
- **mEWS ≥ 4** is the universal trigger threshold for activating the IoT pre-admission pipeline.
- **5G URLLC** provides the **≤ 1 ms** latency that makes real-time ECG streaming from ambulance to cardiologist feasible.
- **Edge AI** (1D-CNN) is what makes the ambulance "smart" — it does the triage locally without depending on the cloud.
- A typical legacy ED-LOS of **~52 min (non-treatment)** can be compressed to **~2 min (residual handover)** — a **~61% reduction** — by the IoT pipeline.
- **Cloud-side RAL (Resource Availability Lookup)** is the unsung hero: it is what reserves the ICU bed and Cath-Lab *before* the patient physically arrives.
- The percentage reduction formula $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{legacy}} \times 100$ is the **single most common numerical** in KTU 14-mark questions on this topic — practice it until automatic.
- Always mention **data security** (HIPAA / ABDM / FHIR compliance) and **human-in-the-loop** in a 14-mark answer to demonstrate mature engineering thinking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enhanced Medicine Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Enhanced Medicine Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Enhanced Medicine Management (EMM)** refers to the integration of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, **embedded sensors**, **wireless communication protocols**, and **cloud-edge computing architectures** to automate, monitor, and optimize the entire pharmaceutical lifecycle — from drug prescription, dispensing, intake verification, adherence tracking, inventory replenishment, to post-consumption physiological feedback.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBMT831), Enhanced Medicine Management is formally defined as:

> *A cyber-physical system (CPS) that combines smart hardware (sensor-laden pill dispensers, RFID/NFC tagged packaging, biometric wearables) with intelligent software (ML-driven adherence prediction, cloud-based Electronic Medical Records integration, real-time alert engines) to ensure the **Five Rights of Medication Administration**: Right Patient, Right Drug, Right Dose, Right Route, and Right Time.*

The system architecture typically consists of four hierarchical tiers:
1. **Perception Layer** — Sensors, RFID tags, load cells, accelerometers
2. **Network Layer** — BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa, MQTT brokers
3. **Processing Layer** — Edge gateways, cloud servers, AI/ML inference engines
4. **Application Layer** — Mobile/Web dashboards, caregiver alert systems, hospital EHR integration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Enhanced Medicine Management is positioned as a direct application of **Module 2 (IoT Architecture & Protocols)** and **Module 3 (Healthcare IoT verticals)**. Expect questions that test both the *application logic* and the *underlying protocol selection rationale*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Enhanced Medicine Management like a **"smart waiter in a hospital restaurant"**:

Imagine a restaurant where every customer (patient) has a personal waiter. The waiter:
- **Remembers** the exact dish (medicine) each customer ordered (prescription)
- **Watches the kitchen** to know when the dish is ready (tracks pharmacy inventory via RFID)
- **Serves the dish at the right time** (dispenses pills at scheduled times)
- **Watches the customer eat** (sensors verify pill removal/intake)
- **Calls the chef if the customer refuses** (alerts caregiver on missed dose)
- **Updates the customer's health file** (logs to cloud EHR)

Before IoT, the waiter was human — prone to forgetting, mixing up orders, and unable to be in two places. **Enhanced Medicine Management is essentially this waiter, but robotic, tireless, and connected to a central command center (cloud)** that coordinates every "restaurant" (patient) across the city (healthcare network).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following metrics are the industry-accepted benchmarks for evaluating an EMM system:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Medication Adherence Rate (MAR)** | $\geq 80\%$ | WHO-defined threshold for therapeutic efficacy |
| **Dose Verification Latency** | $\leq 2$ seconds | Time from pill removal to caregiver notification |
| **System Uptime (SLA)** | $99.9\%$ | Maximum permissible downtime (8.76 hrs/year) |
| **Sensor Sampling Frequency** | $10$–$100$ Hz | For load-cell based pill detection |
| **BLE Communication Range** | $10$ m (Class 2) | Standard for in-home dispensers |
| **Battery Life (portable dispenser)** | $\geq 6$ months | At 1 dispensation event/day |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to memorize:** The WHO Medication Adherence threshold is **80%**. Any system claiming "high adherence" must demonstrate this benchmark statistically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an IoT-Based Enhanced Medicine Management System

The system is decomposed into the following functional blocks, each with a clearly defined role:

### 2.1.1 Perception Layer (Hardware End)

- **Smart Pill Dispenser Module:**
  - Contains $N$ compartments (typically $7$ to $28$ for weekly/monthly cycles)
  - Each compartment equipped with a **load cell** (strain gauge) to detect pill weight change
  - **IR break-beam sensor** or **capacitive proximity sensor** to detect hand insertion
  - **Microcontroller** (ESP32 / Arduino Nano 33 IoT) for local processing
- **RFID/NFC Tagging:**
  - Each pill bottle carries a passive **HF (13.56 MHz)** RFID tag
  - Reader antenna integrated into dispenser lid
  - Anti-counterfeiting verification through cryptographic signature
- **Wearable Biometric Sensor:**
  - Wrist-worn PPG + accelerometer for **post-intake physiological verification**
  - Confirms swallowing via throat EMG or detects adverse reactions

### 2.1.2 Network Layer (Communication)

- **Indoor (Patient ↔ Gateway):** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) — preferred for low power
- **Outdoor (Gateway ↔ Cloud):** Wi-Fi (802.11n) or cellular (NB-IoT / LTE-M)
- **Application Protocol:** **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for lightweight publish-subscribe messaging
- **Data Format:** JSON payloads over TLS 1.3 encrypted channels

### 2.1.3 Processing Layer (Intelligence)

- **Edge Computing:** Real-time decision making (e.g., "Did patient take dose within 30-min window?")
- **Cloud Computing:** Long-term storage, ML model training, population-level analytics
- **ML Models Deployed:**
  - **LSTM** networks for adherence time-series prediction
  - **Random Forest** classifiers for adverse drug reaction (ADR) detection
  - **K-Means clustering** for patient stratification

### 2.1.4 Application Layer (User Interface)

- **Patient App:** Reminders, refill alerts, family share
- **Caregiver Dashboard:** Real-time multi-patient monitoring
- **Pharmacy Integration:** Auto-refill trigger when stock < threshold

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The end-to-end operational flow of an Enhanced Medicine Management system proceeds as follows:

1. **Prescription Ingestion:** Doctor uploads prescription to cloud EHR via HL7 FHIR API.
2. **Schedule Generation:** Cloud algorithm converts prescription to a **time-series dispensing schedule** (e.g., "Patient X: 1 tablet of Metformin at 08:00 and 20:00 daily").
3. **Dispenser Sync:** Schedule pushed to patient's smart dispenser via MQTT.
4. **Reminder Trigger:** At scheduled time, dispenser emits audible buzzer + LED + mobile notification.
5. **Patient Interaction:** Patient opens compartment; load cell registers weight change $\Delta W$.
6. **Verification:** System compares $\Delta W$ against expected weight $W_{expected} \pm \epsilon$. If match, logs **"TAKEN"** event; if mismatch, triggers **"WRONG DOSE"** alert.
7. **Cloud Update:** Event published to MQTT broker → consumed by caregiver dashboard.
8. **Adherence Computation:** Rolling 30-day **Medication Possession Ratio (MPR)** updated.
9. **Predictive Alert:** LSTM model forecasts probability of non-adherence next week; if $> 0.7$, caregiver intervention triggered.
10. **Inventory Management:** Pill count in compartment drops below threshold (e.g., $3$ days supply) → auto-refill order placed to partner pharmacy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams** — memorize the following table thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MAR = \frac{N_{taken}}{N_{prescribed}} \times 100\%$ | Medication Adherence Rate | $\%$ |
| 2 | $MPR = \frac{D_{days\_supplied}}{D_{days\_in\_period}} \times 100\%$ | Medication Possession Ratio | $\%$ |
| 3 | $PDC = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[d_i \geq 0.8]}{N}$ | Proportion of Days Covered | ratio |
| 4 | $E_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt} \times V_{nom}}{I_{avg} \times 86400}$ | Battery life (days) | days |
| 5 | $T_{latency} = T_{sense} + T_{tx} + T_{proc} + T_{cloud}$ | End-to-end latency | seconds |
| 6 | $R_{alert} = \lambda \times P_{non\_adh}$ | Alert rate per patient per day | alerts/day |
| 7 | $S_{storage} = N_{patients} \times E_{events\_day} \times B_{event} \times D_{retention}$ | Cloud storage requirement | bytes |
| 8 | $\eta_{disp} = \frac{N_{correct\_events}}{N_{total\_events}} \times 100\%$ | Dispenser reliability | $\%$ |
| 9 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | RFID read reliability | dB |
| 10 | $T_{res} = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Sensor sampling period | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention used throughout KTU 2024 materials:** $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function, $\lambda$ is the event rate parameter, $f_s$ is sampling frequency.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Enhanced Medicine Management systems are deployed in the following engineering and clinical contexts:

- **Chronic Disease Management:** Diabetes, hypertension, tuberculosis (DOTS program)
- **Geriatric Care:** Assisted living facilities managing polypharmacy (5+ drugs)
- **Clinical Trials:** Phase II/III drug trials requiring strict adherence logging
- **Post-Operative Recovery:** Surgical patients on complex multi-drug regimens
- **Psychiatric Care:** Antipsychotic medication compliance (historically $< 50\%$ without intervention)
- **Veterinary Medicine:** Smart dispensers for pet medication adherence

The economic impact is substantial: the **New England Healthcare Institute** estimates non-adherence costs the US healthcare system **\$290 billion annually**, and IoT-based EMM systems have demonstrated adherence improvement from $50\%$ to $90\%+$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Pill Detection Threshold (Load-Cell Based)

### Problem Context
A smart pill dispenser uses a load cell to detect pill removal. Given the measurement noise characteristics, derive the optimal detection threshold that minimizes both false positives (no pill removed but alarm raised) and false negatives (pill removed but not detected).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the load cell output (in grams) at time $t$ be denoted $w(t)$. The signal model is:

$$w(t) = w_0 + n(t)$$

where $w_0$ is the true pill weight and $n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ is additive Gaussian noise with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2$.

The decision problem is a **binary hypothesis test**:

$$\mathcal{H}_0: \text{No pill removed} \implies w(t) = w_{rest} + n(t)$$

$$\mathcal{H}_1: \text{Pill removed} \implies w(t) = w_{rest} - w_{pill} + n(t)$$

The log-likelihood ratio is:

$$\Lambda(w) = \log\left(\frac{f(w \vert \mathcal{H}_1)}{f(w \vert \mathcal{H}_0)}\right) = \frac{(w - w_{rest} + w_{pill})^2 - (w - w_{rest})^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$\Lambda(w) = \frac{w_{pill}(2w - 2w_{rest} + w_{pill})}{2\sigma^2}$$

Setting $\Lambda(w) = 0$ (equal prior probability) and solving for $w$:

$$2w - 2w_{rest} + w_{pill} = 0$$

$$w_{threshold} = w_{rest} - \frac{w_{pill}}{2}$$

### Result

$$\boxed{w_{threshold} = w_{rest} - \frac{w_{pill}}{2}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The optimal threshold is exactly **halfway between the resting weight and the post-removal weight**. This minimizes the total probability of error for equal-cost, equal-prior scenarios — a direct application of the **Neyman-Pearson lemma** in the equal-prior special case.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a hysteresis band of $\pm 0.1 w_{pill}$ is added to prevent rapid oscillation (chattering) near the threshold.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Life Estimation for Portable Smart Dispenser

### Problem Context
A portable smart pill dispenser uses an ESP32 microcontroller with the following power profile:
- Active mode (dispensing event): $I_{active} = 120$ mA for $t_{active} = 5$ s per event
- Sleep mode (idle): $I_{sleep} = 10$ $\mu$A for the remainder of the day
- Number of dispensing events per day: $n = 4$

A $3.7$ V, $2000$ mAh Li-Po battery powers the system. Compute the operational battery life in days.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Compute average current draw.

$$I_{active\_day} = I_{active} \times \frac{t_{active} \times n}{86400} = 120 \times \frac{5 \times 4}{86400}$$

$$I_{active\_day} = 120 \times 0.000231 = 0.0278 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2:** Total average current.

$$I_{avg} = I_{active\_day} + I_{sleep} = 0.0278 + 0.010 = 0.0378 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3:** Apply battery life formula.

$$E_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{0.0378 \text{ mA}} = 52{,}910 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to days.

$$E_{batt\_days} = \frac{52{,}910}{24} = 2204.6 \text{ days} \approx 6.04 \text{ years}$$

### Result

$$\boxed{E_{batt} \approx 6 \text{ years}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This demonstrates why **sleep mode dominates battery life** in IoT medical devices. A 10,000× current reduction during sleep translates to multi-year operation on a single charge — a critical design constraint for medical-grade devices where battery replacement is operationally expensive.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cloud Storage Sizing for Multi-Patient EMM Deployment

### Problem Context
A hospital plans to deploy Enhanced Medicine Management to $N = 500$ patients. Each patient generates $E = 12$ events per day (6 doses $\times$ 2 events: dispense + verify). Each event record is $B = 256$ bytes. Data retention requirement is $D = 7$ years. Compute the total cloud storage requirement.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** Daily event volume per patient.

$$V_{daily} = E \times B = 12 \times 256 = 3072 \text{ bytes/patient/day}$$

**Step 2:** Annual volume per patient.

$$V_{annual} = V_{daily} \times 365 = 3072 \times 365 = 1{,}121{,}280 \text{ bytes/patient/year}$$

**Step 3:** 7-year volume per patient.

$$V_{7yr} = V_{annual} \times 7 = 1{,}121{,}280 \times 7 = 7{,}848{,}960 \text{ bytes} \approx 7.49 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4:** Total storage for 500 patients.

$$S_{total} = N \times V_{7yr} = 500 \times 7{,}848{,}960 = 3{,}924{,}480{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to GB with 30% overhead for indexing, backups, replication.

$$S_{provisioned} = S_{total} \times 1.3 = 5{,}101{,}824{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 4.75 \text{ GB}$$

### Result

$$\boxed{S_{total} \approx 4.75 \text{ GB (with overhead)}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The storage requirement is modest, demonstrating the **economic feasibility** of EMM systems even at hospital scale. The dominant cost factor is not storage but **secure transmission (TLS encryption overhead)** and **regulatory compliance (HIPAA/GDPR audit logging)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Communication Protocol Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently asked in KTU exams:** "Justify the choice of BLE over Wi-Fi for in-home EMM."

| Criterion | BLE 5.0 | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | LoRaWAN | NB-IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $10$–$100$ m | $50$ m indoor | $2$–$10$ km | $1$–$10$ km |
| **Power Consumption** | Very Low ($\sim 10$ mW) | High ($\sim 1$ W) | Very Low | Low |
| **Data Rate** | $2$ Mbps | $150$ Mbps | $0.3$–$50$ kbps | $26$ kbps |
| **Latency** | $7.5$ ms | $20$ ms | $1$–$10$ s | $1.6$–$10$ s |
| **Best Use Case in EMM** | Patient ↔ Gateway | Gateway ↔ Cloud | Rural patient → Cloud | Mobile patient → Cloud |
| **KTU Recommendation** | $\star\star\star\star\star$ | $\star\star\star$ | $\star\star$ | $\star\star\star$ |

**Justification for BLE as primary in-home protocol:** The data volume per event is small ($\sim 256$ bytes), latency requirement is moderate ($< 2$ s), and the device must run for years on battery — making BLE the optimal choice. Wi-Fi is reserved for the gateway-to-cloud hop where higher bandwidth and existing infrastructure offset power concerns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of Enhanced Medicine Management Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of an IoT-based EMM system, showing data flow from sensor perception to caregiver notification.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Physiological State] --> B[Wearable Biometric Sensor]
    B --> C[BLE Wireless Transmission]
    
    D[Smart Pill Dispenser] --> E[Load Cell Sensor]
    D --> F[IR Break Beam Sensor]
    D --> G[RFID Reader Module]
    
    E --> H[ESP32 Microcontroller]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    
    H --> I[Local Edge Processing]
    I --> J{Schedule Match?}
    
    J -->|Yes| K[Log Taken Event]
    J -->|No| L[Trigger Buzzer and LED]
    
    K --> C
    L --> C
    
    C --> M[MQTT Broker Gateway]
    
    M --> N[Cloud Server]
    N --> O[Adherence Database]
    N --> P[LSTM Prediction Engine]
    N --> Q[Inventory Management Module]
    
    P --> R{Risk of Non Adherence > 70 percent}
    R -->|Yes| S[Send Caregiver Alert]
    R -->|No| T[Continue Monitoring]
    
    Q --> U{Stock Below Threshold}
    U -->|Yes| V[Auto Refill Order to Pharmacy]
    U -->|No| T
    
    S --> W[Caregiver Mobile Dashboard]
    V --> X[Partner Pharmacy System]
    O --> Y[Hospital EHR via FHIR API]
    
    W --> Z[Family Member Notification]
    Y --> AA[Physician Review Interface]
```

## 4.2 Data Flow Sequence Diagram — Complete Medication Cycle

This sequence diagram illustrates the temporal interaction between system components during a single medication event, a common KTU exam question.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pat as Patient
    participant Disp as Smart Dispenser
    participant GW as BLE Gateway
    participant Cloud as Cloud Server
    participant CG as Caregiver
    participant Pharm as Pharmacy

    Note over Disp: Scheduled time arrives
    Disp->>Pat: Buzzer + LED + Mobile notification
    Pat->>Disp: Opens compartment
    Disp->>Disp: Load cell reads weight delta
    Disp->>Disp: Verify weight against expected
    alt Weight matches within tolerance
        Disp->>GW: Publish TAKEN event via BLE
        GW->>Cloud: Forward to MQTT broker
        Cloud->>Cloud: Update adherence log
        Cloud->>CG: Optional confirmation
    else Weight mismatch
        Disp->>GW: Publish WRONG_DOSE alert
        GW->>Cloud: Forward critical alert
        Cloud->>CG: Immediate push notification
        Cloud->>Pharm: Log incident for review
    end

    Note over Cloud: Continuous monitoring
    Cloud->>Cloud: LSTM predicts next 7 day adherence
    alt Predicted risk above 70 percent
        Cloud->>CG: Risk alert with patient ID
    end

    Note over Cloud: Daily inventory check
    alt Stock below 3 day threshold
        Cloud->>Pharm: Auto refill request
        Pharm->>Cloud: Order confirmation
    end
```

## 4.3 State Machine — Pill Dispenser Operational States

This diagram captures all valid states of the dispenser hardware, useful for embedded systems exam questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ReminderActive: Schedule time reached
    ReminderActive --> PatientInteraction: Sensor detects compartment open
    ReminderActive --> MissedDose: 30 minute timeout elapsed
    PatientInteraction --> WeightVerifying: Load cell reads delta W
    WeightVerifying --> DoseAccepted: Delta W matches expected
    WeightVerifying --> DoseRejected: Delta W outside tolerance
    DoseAccepted --> EventLogged: Publish to MQTT
    DoseRejected --> CaregiverAlert: Immediate notification
    EventLogged --> Idle: Return to sleep mode
    CaregiverAlert --> Idle: Alert acknowledged
    MissedDose --> CaregiverAlert: Log missed event
    Idle --> MaintenanceMode: Button held 5 seconds
    MaintenanceMode --> Idle: Refill complete
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Matrix

| Component | Communicates With | Protocol | Data Frequency | Critical Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Load Cell | ESP32 MCU | Analog ADC | $10$ Hz | Yes |
| IR Sensor | ESP32 MCU | GPIO Interrupt | Event-driven | Yes |
| RFID Reader | ESP32 MCU | SPI | On-demand | No |
| ESP32 MCU | BLE Gateway | BLE 5.0 | Event-driven | Yes |
| BLE Gateway | Cloud Server | MQTT over TLS | Per event | Yes |
| Cloud Server | Caregiver App | HTTPS Push | Real-time | Yes |
| Cloud Server | Pharmacy | REST API | Daily | No |
| Wearable | BLE Gateway | BLE | $1$ Hz | No |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
Define **Enhanced Medicine Management (EMM)** in the context of IoT-based healthcare systems. List any **four functional components** of an EMM system.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Enhanced Medicine Management is an IoT-enabled cyber-physical system that integrates smart sensors, wireless communication, and cloud analytics to automate, monitor, and verify medication prescription, dispensing, intake, and adherence — ensuring the **Five Rights of Medication Administration** (Right Patient, Drug, Dose, Route, Time).

**Four Functional Components (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Smart Pill Dispenser** — Sensor-laden hardware for automated dispensing
2. **Adherence Monitoring Engine** — Cloud/edge algorithms for tracking intake
3. **Communication Gateway** — BLE/Wi-Fi/MQTT infrastructure
4. **Caregiver/Patient Dashboard** — Mobile/web interface for alerts and analytics

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
What is the significance of the **WHO 80% adherence threshold** in evaluating an IoT-based medicine management system? How is **Medication Adherence Rate (MAR)** computed?

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Significance (1.5 Marks):**
The WHO has established that medication adherence rates **below 80%** are associated with sub-therapeutic outcomes, increased hospitalizations, and disease progression. For chronic conditions like hypertension, diabetes, and tuberculosis, achieving and sustaining adherence $\geq 80\%$ is the **primary clinical efficacy benchmark** for any EMM intervention. The threshold serves as a **design specification target** and **regulatory approval criterion** for IoT-based adherence solutions.

**MAR Formula (1.5 Marks):**

$$MAR = \frac{N_{taken}}{N_{prescribed}} \times 100\%$$

where $N_{taken}$ is the number of correctly taken doses and $N_{prescribed}$ is the total number of prescribed doses over the evaluation period (typically 30, 60, or 90 days).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned with 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-layer architecture** of an IoT-based Enhanced Medicine Management system. Discuss the role of each layer and identify two key technologies/components in each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A smart pill dispenser uses a load cell with measurement noise standard deviation $\sigma = 0.05$ g. The resting weight of a compartment is $w_{rest} = 12.00$ g and the pill weight is $w_{pill} = 0.50$ g. Determine the **optimal detection threshold** and compute the **probability of false alarm** assuming Gaussian noise. Use the standard normal table. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Layer 1: Perception Layer (1.75 Marks)**
- **Role:** Physical data acquisition from the patient and medication environment
- **Technologies:** Load cells (strain gauges) for pill weight detection, RFID readers for pill bottle identification, IR break-beam sensors for hand detection, PPG sensors on wearable for physiological verification

**Layer 2: Network Layer (1.75 Marks)**
- **Role:** Reliable, secure data transmission from perception layer to processing layer
- **Technologies:** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE 5.0) for patient-to-gateway communication, Wi-Fi (802.11n) for gateway-to-cloud, MQTT protocol for lightweight publish-subscribe messaging, TLS 1.3 for end-to-end encryption

**Layer 3: Processing Layer (1.75 Marks)**
- **Role:** Data aggregation, intelligent decision-making, and predictive analytics
- **Technologies:** Edge microcontrollers (ESP32) for local decision logic, cloud servers (AWS IoT/Azure IoT Hub) for centralized storage, LSTM neural networks for adherence prediction, REST APIs for system integration

**Layer 4: Application Layer (1.75 Marks)**
- **Role:** End-user interaction and clinical integration
- **Technologies:** Mobile applications (React Native) for patient reminders, web dashboards for caregivers, HL7 FHIR APIs for EHR integration, automated pharmacy refill systems

**(b) Optimal Threshold & Probability Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: State the hypothesis test (2 Marks)**
- $\mathcal{H}_0$ (no pill): $w \sim \mathcal{N}(w_{rest}, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(12.00, 0.05^2)$
- $\mathcal{H}_1$ (pill removed): $w \sim \mathcal{N}(w_{rest} - w_{pill}, \sigma^2) = \mathcal{N}(11.50, 0.05^2)$

**Step 2: Compute optimal threshold (2 Marks)**

$$w_{threshold} = w_{rest} - \frac{w_{pill}}{2} = 12.00 - \frac{0.50}{2} = 11.75 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Compute false alarm probability (3 Marks)**

False alarm occurs when $\mathcal{H}_0$ is true but $w < w_{threshold}$:

$$P_{FA} = P(w < 11.75 \vert \mathcal{H}_0) = P\left(Z < \frac{11.75 - 12.00}{0.05}\right)$$

$$P_{FA} = P(Z < -5.0) = \Phi(-5.0) \approx 2.87 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Conclusion:** $P_{FA} \approx 2.87 \times 10^{-7}$, which is essentially negligible — demonstrating the high reliability of load-cell-based pill detection in EMM systems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Five Rights of Medication Administration** and describe how each right is **technologically enforced** in an IoT-based Enhanced Medicine Management system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A portable smart pill dispenser operates with the following power profile: $I_{active} = 150$ mA during dispensing (duration $3$ s, occurring $6$ times per day), $I_{sleep} = 15$ $\mu$A otherwise. The battery is rated $3.7$ V, $2500$ mAh. Calculate the **total average current** and the **expected battery life in years**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five Rights Enforced by IoT (7 Marks — 1.4 Marks Each Right)**

1. **Right Patient:** Enforced via **biometric authentication** (fingerprint sensor on dispenser lid) and **RFID wristband** matching against the prescription record. The dispenser refuses to open until the patient's identity is verified.

2. **Right Drug:** Enforced via **RFID-tagged medication packaging**. When the compartment is loaded, the RFID reader scans the tag and cross-references with the prescription database. A mismatch triggers a **lockout and alert**.

3. **Right Dose:** Enforced via **load-cell weight verification**. The system measures the weight of pills removed and compares against the expected dose. Discrepancies above $\pm 5\%$ trigger caregiver notification.

4. **Right Route:** Enforced via **smart packaging indicators** and **app-based patient education modules**. The dispenser displays the route (oral, sublingual, topical) via LED color-coding and audio instructions.

5. **Right Time:** Enforced via **scheduled dispensing with tolerance window** (typically $\pm 15$ minutes). The dispenser remains locked outside the window. Late or missed doses are logged and trigger escalation alerts.

**(b) Battery Life Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute active current contribution per day (2 Marks)**

$$I_{active\_day} = I_{active} \times \frac{t_{active} \times n}{86400} = 150 \times \frac{3 \times 6}{86400}$$

$$I_{active\_day} = 150 \times 0.000208 = 0.03125 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2: Compute sleep current contribution per day (1.5 Marks)**

$$I_{sleep\_day} = I_{sleep} = 0.015 \text{ mA} \text{ (continuous)}$$

**Step 3: Total average current (1.5 Marks)**

$$I_{avg} = I_{active\_day} + I_{sleep} = 0.03125 + 0.015 = 0.04625 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4: Battery life calculation (2 Marks)**

$$E_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2500 \text{ mAh}}{0.04625 \text{ mA}} = 54{,}054 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5: Convert to years (0.5 Marks)**

$$E_{batt\_years} = \frac{54{,}054}{24 \times 365} = \frac{54{,}054}{8760} \approx 6.17 \text{ years}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{E_{batt} \approx 6.17 \text{ years}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert seconds to hours when computing average current. Always normalize to the same time unit (seconds or hours) before dividing.
> - **Confusing $I_{sleep}$:** Sleep current is in $\mu$A (micro-amps) — must be converted to mA (dividing by 1000) before adding to active current. A common error yields answers off by $1000\times$.
> - **Skipping the threshold derivation:** In question (a) of Question A, students often state the formula $w_{threshold} = w_{rest} - w_{pill}/2$ without showing the log-likelihood ratio derivation. **Always show the derivation** for full marks in KTU 2024 Scheme.
> - **Ignoring practical hysteresis:** Real systems need a hysteresis band; mentioning this earns bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these points for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

- ✅ **Definition:** EMM = IoT + sensors + cloud + analytics for automated medication management ensuring the **Five Rights**.
- ✅ **Architecture:** Four layers — Perception, Network, Processing, Application.
- ✅ **Five Rights:** Right Patient, Drug, Dose, Route, Time.
- ✅ **Key Sensor:** Load cell for weight-based pill detection; optimal threshold = $w_{rest} - w_{pill}/2$.
- ✅ **Primary Protocol:** BLE 5.0 for in-home, Wi-Fi/MQTT for cloud hop.
- ✅ **Adherence Metric:** $MAR = \frac{N_{taken}}{N_{prescribed}} \times 100\%$; WHO threshold = $80\%$.
- ✅ **Other Metrics:** MPR (Medication Possession Ratio), PDC (Proportion of Days Covered).
- ✅ **Battery Life Formula:** $E_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}}$ in hours.
- ✅ **Latency Budget:** End-to-end dose verification latency must be $\leq 2$ seconds.
- ✅ **Storage Estimate:** $\sim 10$ MB/patient/year for typical EMM event logs.
- ✅ **ML Models:** LSTM for adherence prediction, Random Forest for ADR detection.
- ✅ **Standards:** HL7 FHIR for EHR integration, TLS 1.3 for security, MQTT for messaging.
- ✅ **Cost Impact:** Non-adherence costs US \$290B/year; EMM improves adherence from 50% to 90%+.
- ✅ **Deployment Domains:** Chronic disease, geriatrics, clinical trials, psychiatry, veterinary.
- ✅ **Three Clinical Verticals:** Diabetes, hypertension, TB (DOTS program) are the most common EMM targets.
- ✅ **Power Profile Dominance:** Sleep current dominates; optimization focuses on $I_{sleep}$ reduction.
- ✅ **Edge vs Cloud Split:** Time-critical decisions at edge; analytics and long-term storage in cloud.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** Neyman-Pearson lemma yields the load-cell threshold under Gaussian noise.
- ✅ **Hysteresis:** Always add a $\pm 0.1 w_{pill}$ band to prevent chattering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Beds for Patients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT831 - IOT & BIOMEDICAL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Beds for Patients**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Bed for Patients** is an IoT-enabled, sensor-integrated medical bed system designed to continuously monitor, record, and transmit critical physiological and postural parameters of a patient lying on it. It uses a heterogeneous network of embedded sensors (pressure, load, temperature, humidity, and motion sensors) coupled with microcontrollers, wireless transceivers, and cloud-edge analytics to deliver real-time alerts regarding patient safety, comfort, pressure-ulcer prevention, bed-exit events, and vital signs such as heart rate and respiratory rate.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the smart bed is positioned as a flagship **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** in healthcare, where physical sensing of patient state is fused with cyber computation (cloud/edge AI) to enable **ambient assisted living (AAL)** and clinical decision support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OEBMT831 - Module 3):** Smart beds integrate **sensing layer + communication layer + analytics layer** into a single bedside IoT node. They are evaluated under *CO3: Design IoT architectures for biomedical applications*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an ordinary hospital bed is a **dumb mattress** — it simply holds the patient. Now imagine a **"mattress with a nervous system"** that can:
- Feel every body part pressing against it
- Sense when the patient turns, leaves, or stops breathing
- Know if the sheet is wet
- Whisper this information wirelessly to a nurse's phone or a doctor's dashboard

**Analogy:** Think of the smart bed as a **"Fitbit that never leaves the patient and never needs charging by the patient."** Just as a smartwatch monitors steps and heart rate, the smart bed continuously monitors pressure distribution, micro-movements, vitals, and posture — but without the patient having to wear anything.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics and Standard Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Interface Pressure Threshold** | **< 32 mmHg** | Above this, capillary occlusion begins (NPUAP/EPUAP standard for pressure ulcer prevention) |
| **Sampling Rate (Pressure Mat)** | **10–100 Hz** | Required for capturing weight shifts and BCG signal |
| **Load Cell Capacity** | **150–250 kg** | Standard ICU bed capacity |
| **Wireless Protocol** | **BLE 5.0 / Wi-Fi 802.11n / Zigbee 3.0** | Trade-off between range, power, and bandwidth |
| **Operating Temperature** | **10 °C – 40 °C** | Hospital ambient standard |
| **Data Latency (Alert Path)** | **< 2 seconds** | Critical for fall prevention alerts |
| **Battery Backup** | **≥ 8 hours** | Uninterrupted monitoring during power failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Capillary Occlusion Pressure:** When external pressure on skin exceeds the average capillary blood pressure (~32 mmHg at the arteriolar end), blood flow is cut off, leading to tissue ischemia and pressure ulcers (bedsores) within 2–6 hours if unrelieved.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture (Three-Layer IoT Stack)

A smart bed is best understood as a **three-tier IoT architecture**:

### Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Sensors)
- **Piezoresistive / FSR (Force Sensitive Resistor) array** — pressure mapping
- **Load cells (Wheatstone bridge)** — gross body weight
- **MEMS accelerometer (3-axis)** — patient turn/movement
- **DHT22 / NTC thermistor** — skin/sheet temperature
- **Capacitive humidity sensor** — incontinence/moisture
- **Piezo film / EMFi sensor** — ballistocardiography (BCG) for heart rate
- **Ultrasonic / PIR** — bed-exit detection

### Layer 2 — Network Layer
- **Microcontroller (ESP32 / STM32 / nRF52840)**
- **Wireless transceivers:** BLE for low-power nurse-dashboard sync, Wi-Fi for cloud upload, LoRaWAN for multi-bed ward telemetry
- **MQTT / CoAP** as lightweight application protocols

### Layer 3 — Application Layer
- **Edge gateway → Cloud (AWS IoT / Azure HealthLake)**
- **ML models** for pressure ulcer risk (Braden scale + sensor fusion)
- **Mobile/Web dashboard** for nurse/doctor alerts

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pressure Mapping Principle** — The patient lies on a matrix of FSR cells. Each cell's resistance decreases as force increases.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — A voltage divider converts resistance change to voltage, which is amplified and digitized via ADC.
3. **Posture Classification** — A CNN/LSTM running on edge processes the pressure image to identify supine, lateral-left, lateral-right, prone, or sitting-up.
4. **Vital Sign Extraction (BCG)** — The mechanical recoil of the body with each heartbeat produces tiny forces on the bed; the EMFi/piezo film captures this and bandpass filters (0.5–10 Hz) to extract heart rate.
5. **Pressure Ulcer Risk Engine** — Computes the cumulative time any cell exceeds 32 mmHg and raises an alert if unrelieved > 2 h.
6. **Bed-Exit Algorithm** — A sudden drop in total load below a threshold (e.g., 10 kg) combined with accelerometer activity triggers an exit alarm.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure from Force | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area of contact | Pa (N/m²) |
| mmHg to Pascal | $1 \text{ mmHg} \approx 133.322 \text{ Pa}$ | Clinical pressure conversion | Pa |
| FSR Resistance Model | $R_{FSR} \approx \dfrac{k}{F^{\alpha}}$ | $k$, $\alpha$ are sensor constants; inverse power law | $\Omega$ |
| Voltage Divider Output | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{FSR}}{R_{FSR} + R_{ref}}$ | Sensor readout | V |
| Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_{o} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_3}{R_3+R_4} - \dfrac{R_2}{R_2+R_1} \right)$ | Load cell output voltage | V |
| Gauge Factor | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | Strain gauge sensitivity | dimensionless |
| Capacitive Sensor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Humidity / pressure capacitance | F |
| Heart Rate from BCG (Peak Detection) | $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{peak}}$ | Time between successive BCG J-peaks | bpm |
| Respiration Rate | $RR = \dfrac{60}{T_{breath}}$ | Time between successive inhalation cycles | breaths/min |
| Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling for BCG (~10 Hz signal) | Hz |
| MQTT QoS Latency | $L_{total} = L_{publish} + L_{broker} + L_{subscribe}$ | End-to-end alert latency | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why mmHg matters clinically:** Capillary closing pressure is universally cited as **32 mmHg** (≈ **4.27 kPa**). Any sensor-cell pressure sustained above this for **> 2 hours** statistically correlates with **Stage I–II pressure ulcer formation**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ICU & Step-down Units:** Continuous APACHE-score-augmenting monitoring without nurses attaching electrodes.
- **Long-term Care / Geriatric Wards:** Fall prevention (alerts in < 2 s) reduces hip-fracture mortality.
- **Home Care / Sleep Labs:** Sleep staging, apnea detection, and cardiac monitoring without polysomnography overhead.
- **Rehabilitation:** Posture compliance tracking after spinal surgery.
- **COVID/Quarantine Wards:** Zero-contact vitals monitoring reduced PPE consumption globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure at a Sensor Cell from FSR Reading

**Given:** A piezoresistive FSR is connected in a voltage divider with $R_{ref} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{cc} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. The ADC reads $V_{out} = 1.2 \text{ V}$. The sensor obeys $R_{FSR} = k / F^{\alpha}$ with $k = 5 \times 10^{6}$ and $\alpha = 0.85$. The active cell area is $A = 1 \text{ cm}^{2} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Solve for $R_{FSR}$ from voltage divider:**

$$
V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{FSR}}{R_{FSR} + R_{ref}}
$$

$$
1.2 = 3.3 \cdot \frac{R_{FSR}}{R_{FSR} + 10{,}000}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $R_{FSR}$:**

$$
\frac{1.2}{3.3} = \frac{R_{FSR}}{R_{FSR} + 10{,}000}
$$

$$
0.3636 \cdot (R_{FSR} + 10{,}000) = R_{FSR}
$$

$$
0.3636 \cdot R_{FSR} + 3636 = R_{FSR}
$$

$$
3636 = R_{FSR} - 0.3636 \cdot R_{FSR} = R_{FSR} \cdot (1 - 0.3636)
$$

$$
R_{FSR} = \frac{3636}{0.6364} \approx 5713 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Convert $R_{FSR}$ to Force using inverse model:**

$$
F^{\alpha} = \frac{k}{R_{FSR}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{5713} \approx 875.3
$$

$$
F = (875.3)^{1/0.85} = (875.3)^{1.1765}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power:**

$$
F \approx 875.3^{1.1765}
$$

Take $\log_{10}$:

$$
\log_{10}(F) = 1.1765 \cdot \log_{10}(875.3) = 1.1765 \cdot 2.9421 \approx 3.461
$$

$$
F \approx 10^{3.461} \approx 2890 \text{ N (in arbitrary model units; real calibration needed)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute pressure and convert to mmHg:**

$$
P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{2890}{10^{-4}} = 2.89 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}
$$

> In a *real* device, calibration maps voltage → pressure in mmHg directly. The point of this derivation is the **method chain**: voltage → resistance → force → pressure → clinical mmHg.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold-Based Pressure Ulcer Alert (Time-Integral)

**Clinical question:** How long must a cell exceed 32 mmHg before an alert fires?

**Step 1 — Define the pressure-time integral (PTI):**

$$
PTI(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \max\left(0,\ P_{cell}(\tau) - P_{crit}\right) d\tau
$$

where $P_{crit} = 32 \text{ mmHg}$.

**Step 2 — Discretize for embedded implementation (sample period $\Delta t$):**

$$
PTI[n] = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \max(0,\ P[i] - 32) \cdot \Delta t
$$

**Step 3 — Define alert threshold empirically** (from NPUAP guidelines and Braden scale mapping):

$$
PTI_{alert} = (P_{avg} - 32) \cdot T_{alert}
$$

For $P_{avg} = 60$ mmHg, $T_{alert} = 2$ h = 7200 s:

$$
PTI_{alert} = (60 - 32) \cdot 7200 = 28 \cdot 7200 = 201{,}600 \text{ mmHg·s}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule for firmware:**

$$
\text{Alert} = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } PTI[n] \geq 201{,}600 \text{ mmHg·s} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heart Rate from BCG Peak Detection

**Given:** A piezo film under the mattress records BCG signal. Three successive J-peaks (cardiac ejection peaks) occur at $t_1 = 0.000$ s, $t_2 = 0.857$ s, $t_3 = 1.692$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute Inter-Beat Intervals (IBI):**

$$
IBI_1 = t_2 - t_1 = 0.857 \text{ s}
$$

$$
IBI_2 = t_3 - t_2 = 0.835 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Average IBI:**

$$
\overline{IBI} = \frac{0.857 + 0.835}{2} = 0.846 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to BPM:**

$$
HR = \frac{60}{\overline{IBI}} = \frac{60}{0.846} \approx 70.92 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Step 4 — Clinical sanity check:** Normal adult resting HR is 60–100 bpm. Result 70.9 bpm is **physiologically valid** → no alert.

> [!NOTE]
> **Filter requirement:** The BCG signal is buried in motion artifacts (0.1–0.5 Hz body sway). A **bandpass filter 0.5–10 Hz** is mandatory *before* peak detection. A common choice is a 4th-order Butterworth IIR.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Choice vs. Parameter

| Parameter | Best Sensor | Reason | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gross weight | Load cell (4-cell bridge) | High accuracy (±0.1%) | Mechanical retrofit complexity |
| Pressure map | FSR matrix / capacitive textile | Spatial resolution | Lower accuracy at high pressure |
| Heart rate (BCG) | EMFi / PVDF piezo film | Sub-mm displacement sensitivity | Sensitive to mattress type |
| Respiration | Strain gauge on frame / piezo | Detects chest expansion | Calibrates with posture |
| Bed-exit | PIR + load cell sum | Redundancy prevents false alarms | PIR has blind spots |
| Moisture | Capacitive humidity strip | Detects urine within 30 s | Reusable vs. disposable |
| Temperature | NTC thermistor array | ±0.1 °C accuracy, low cost | Slow response (≈10 s) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Body] --> B1[Pressure Mat FS Sensors]
    A --> B2[Load Cells 4 corners]
    A --> B3[MEMS Accelerometer 3 axis]
    A --> B4[Piezo Film BCG]
    A --> B5[Temp and Humidity Sensors]
    B1 --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[MCU ESP32 ADC GPIO]
    D --> E1[BLE to Nurse Tablet]
    D --> E2[WiFi to Cloud MQTT Broker]
    D --> E3[LoRa to Ward Gateway]
    E1 --> F1[Local Dashboard]
    E2 --> F2[Cloud Analytics ML Engine]
    E3 --> F3[Central Nurse Station]
    F2 --> G1[Pressure Ulcer Risk Score]
    F2 --> G2[Vitals Trend Graph]
    F2 --> G3[Fall and Exit Alert]
    G1 --> H[Alert SMS Mobile]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

## 4.2 Smart Bed Data Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Patient
    participant S as Sensor Array
    participant M as Microcontroller
    participant G as Edge Gateway
    participant C as Cloud Server
    participant N as Nurse Mobile App
    P->>S: Applies force and motion
    S->>M: Analog voltage signals
    M->>M: ADC sampling at 50 Hz
    M->>M: Bandpass filter and peak detect
    M->>G: MQTT publish payload JSON
    G->>C: HTTPS TLS encrypted upload
    C->>C: Run ML pressure ulcer model
    C->>N: Push notification if alert
    N-->>M: Acknowledge and silence alert
    M->>S: Continue real time monitoring
```

## 4.3 Pressure Ulcer Decision Logic (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Sampling Loop] --> B[Read 16x32 Pressure Matrix]
    B --> C{Any cell over 32 mmHg}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D[Increment PTI counter]
    D --> E{PTI over threshold}
    E -- No --> A
    E -- Yes --> F[Classify posture using CNN]
    F --> G[Send Turn Reminder to Nurse]
    G --> H[Log event to Cloud]
    H --> A
```

## 4.4 Sensor-to-Parameter Mapping (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PerceptionLayer[Perception Layer]
        S1[FSR Array]
        S2[Load Cell Bridge]
        S3[Accelerometer]
```
